## Codegic Khatim PKI Server

- **Reference ID**: Codegic Khatim PKI Server
- **Title**: Codegic Khatim PKI Server
- **Authors**: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Software
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Khatim PKI Server v5.0 introduces experimental support for the Dilithium PQC algorithm to generate CA certificates, CRLs, OCSP responses, and signatures.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computing breaking traditional cryptographic methods; quantum attacks on RSA and ECC
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Migration process expected to take years involving extensive updates to software and hardware infrastructures worldwide
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Muhammad Wahaj Khan (Author); David Isaac (Author of related posts)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Khatim PKI Server; Khatim OCSP Server; Khatim Sign Server; Khatim TSA Server; Khatim Verify Server; KhatimDoc; PKI Insights; Khatim RA Server; Khatim E-Passport Server; Khatim Trust Suite
- **Protocols Covered**: X.509; OCSP; PKCS#1; PAdES; XAdES; CAdES; JAdES; ASic
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI; HSM; Key Vault; Certificate Provider; Timestamp Server; Sign Server; Verification engine
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; CSRC
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC; RSA-2048
- **Key Takeaways**: Khatim PKI Server v5.0 enables experimental generation of Dilithium-based CA certificates, CRLs, and OCSP responses; Windows OS currently cannot verify PQC certificates generated by the server; Organizations can configure policies to generate End Entity certificates using Dilithium via SERVER or CLIENT_CSR key control; Future support for PAdES, XAdES, CAdES, JAdES, and ASic signature formats is planned; NIST has announced the planned deprecation of RSA-2048 as part of the transition to post-quantum cryptography
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Dilithium 2; Dilithium 3; Dilithium 5; OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.17; OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.18; OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.3.19
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Dilithium noted for small key/signature sizes; no concrete byte values provided
- **Target Audience**: PKI Administrator; Security Architect; Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Khatim PKI Server v5.0; OpenSSL client tool; PostMan or business application for certification requests; Key Vault access for generating Dilithium key pairs
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, pki-workshop, code-signing, hsm-pqc, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected

- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: migration process expected to take years; extensive updates to software and hardware infrastructures required; experimental support for PQC algorithms; Windows OS currently doesn't support PQC algorithms hence certificates can't be verified; phased rollout via CA Certificates, CRLs, OCSP Responses, Timestamp Responses, End Entity Certificates, and PKCS#1 Signatures
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected

## BERTEN MLDS-B235

- **Reference ID**: BERTEN MLDS-B235
- **Title**: BERTEN MLDS-B235
- **Authors**: Hardware Security and Semiconductors
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The MLDS-B235 IP Core implements the NIST Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard (ML-DSA) derived from CRYSTALS-Dilithium for FPGA, SoC, and ASIC technologies.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: large-scale quantum computers
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: MLDS-B235; ML-KEM Crypto Core; PQC ML-DSA Crypto Core; Owlet RNG Analyzer
- **Protocols Covered**: MACsec; IPsec; TLS; SSL
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public Key Infrastructures
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204; FIPS 140-3
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA3-256; AES-192; AES-256; SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: The MLDS-B235 IP Core supports NIST security levels 2, 3, and 5 via ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, and ML-DSA-87 parameter sets; The core includes specific countermeasures against Side-Channel Attacks (SCA), timing, and Simple Power Analysis (SPA); Hedged variant signing is supported to improve message data confidentiality using a random seed; The IP Core is portable to AMD (Xilinx), Intel (Altera), and Microchip (Microsemi) devices with AMBA AXI interfaces.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Security Level 2 (ML-DSA-44); Security Level 3 (ML-DSA-65); Security Level 5 (ML-DSA-87)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Key Generation: 16,200 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 10,250 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 7,100 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Signing Average: 1,650 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 1,000 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 850 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Verifying: 11,150 op/s (ML-DSA-44), 7,600 op/s (ML-DSA-65), 5,200 op/s (ML-DSA-87); Resource usage: 10.2K LUT / 8.1K Register (AMD Xilinx Spartan-7); 18.6K LE / 8.0K Register (Intel Altera MAX 10)
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; Hardware Engineer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AMD (Xilinx), Intel (Altera), or Microchip (Microsemi) device; AMBA AXI interfaces; ANSI C drivers; encrypted netlist for one device family
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Compliance, digital-id, entropy-randomness, hsm-pqc
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: Side-Channel Attacks (SCA), timing attacks, Simple Power Analysis (SPA)
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: FIPS 204 compliance, FIPS 140-3 compliance, Simulation Model
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: Random seed for Hedged variant signing algorithm
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: FPGA (AMD Xilinx Spartan-7, Artix-7, Kintex-7, Virtex-7, Ultrascale, Ultrascale+, Versal ACAP), Intel Altera (MAX 10, Cyclone 10 LP, Cyclone V, Arria 10, Stratix V), ASIC technologies
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Encrypted netlist delivery, perpetual Site Licence, 12 months maintenance and integration support
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: AMBA AXI interfaces, user-programmable register map, ANSI C drivers for fast integration
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: record-now, decrypt-later attack; large-scale quantum computers
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite (CNSA) 2.0 for National Security System owners and operators
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jake Massimo: Applied Scientist on the AWS Cryptography team focusing on post-quantum migration; Nevine Ebeid: Senior Applied Scientist at AWS Cryptography focusing on algorithms development, machine-level optimizations, and FIPS 140-3 requirements
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: AWS-LC; s2n-tls; aws-lc-rs; ACCP 2.0; rustls
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; Cloud KMS; PKI

- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; Diffie-Hellman (DH); Elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH); Curve x25519; EdDSA; Ed25519; SHA-3; SHAKE; AES-256-GCM; AES-256-XTS; SecP256r1
- **Key Takeaways**: AWS-LC FIPS 3.0 is the first open-source module with FIPS 140-3 validated ML-KEM support; Organizations should adopt hybrid key exchange combining ECDH or X25519 with ML-KEM to mitigate record-now, decrypt-later attacks; ML-KEM-768 is recommended for general use while ML-KEM-1024 supports CNSA 2.0 requirements; Performance improvements include up to 94% faster RSA signatures and 360% faster AES-256-XTS encryption on specific hardware
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST security category 1 (ML-KEM-512); NIST security category 3 (ML-KEM-768); NIST security category 5 (ML-KEM-1024); ML-KEM-512 public key 800 bytes; ML-KEM-768 public key 1184 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 public key 1568 bytes
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid key exchange approach; hybrid post-quantum cipher suites; X25519MLKEM768; SecP256r1MLKEM768; hybrid ECDHE-ML-KEM key agreement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-KEM-512 public key 800 bytes, private key 1632 bytes, ciphertext 768 bytes; ML-KEM-768 public key 1184 bytes, private key 2400 bytes, ciphertext 1088 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 public key 1568 bytes, private key 3168 bytes, ciphertext 1568 bytes; RSA signatures on Graviton2 81 percent faster for 2048-bit; EdDSA signing throughput increased by average of 108 percent; AES-256-GCM increase on average 115 percent; AES-256-XTS encrypting 256 B input 360 percent faster on Intel Ice Lake
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Compliance Officer; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AWS-LC FIPS 3.0 module; s2n-tls library; enable X25519MLKEM768 or SecP256r1MLKEM768 curves; Intel 3rd Gen Xeon for AVX512 IFMA instructions
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Compliance, Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, kms-pqc

- **Main Topic**: Cisco IOS XE 17.x configuration guide for implementing quantum-safe encryption using Postquantum Preshared Keys (PPK) via RFC 8784 and SKIP in IKEv2 and IPsec VPNs.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers solving Diffie-Hellman and Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman problems in polynomial time
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected

- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI; Key Management; Certificate Authority; Secure Device Provisioning; Trustpool Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: None detected
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Common Criteria; Suite B
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; Diffie-Hellman; Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman
- **Key Takeaways**: PPK feature is available from Cisco IOS XE Release 17.12.1a and 17.11.1a on specific router platforms; Implementation requires RFC 8784 compliance for IKEv2/IPsec quantum-safe encryption; Dynamic PPKs require integration with a key source sending keys in hex format via SKIP; Feature supports FlexVPN and DMVPN but excludes GETVPN; Quantum computers threaten current DH and ECDH algorithms by solving them in polynomial time
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Network Engineer; Security Architect; Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Cisco IOS XE Release 17.12.1a or 17.11.1a; Supported platforms including Cisco 1000 Series, Catalyst 8000V, Catalyst 8500/8300, ASR 1000; Key source must send key format in hex when using SKIP

## Apple PQ3 / CoreCrypto

- **Reference ID**: Apple PQ3 / CoreCrypto
- **Title**: Apple PQ3 / CoreCrypto
- **Authors**: Secure Messaging and Communication
- **Publication Date**: 2026-02-11
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Apple introduces PQ3, a Level 3 post-quantum cryptographic protocol for iMessage featuring hybrid design and periodic rekeying to mitigate quantum threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later; future quantum computer attacks on classical algorithms
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Rollout starts with iOS 17.4, iPadOS 17.4, macOS 14.4, and watchOS 10.4; full replacement within all supported conversations this year
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Professor David Basin (head of Information Security Group at ETH Zürich, inventor of Tamarin); Professor Douglas Stebila (University of Waterloo researcher on post-quantum security); Felix Linker; Dr. Ralf Sasse
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PQ3; CryptoKit; Tamarin
- **Protocols Covered**: PQ3; PQXDH; iMessage protocol
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Secure Enclave; Apple servers
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; Elliptic Curve cryptography (ECC); Diffie-Hellman; Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH)
- **Key Takeaways**: PQ3 achieves Level 3 security by securing both initial key establishment and ongoing message exchange with post-quantum algorithms; Hybrid design combines ML-KEM with ECC to ensure security is never less than the existing classical protocol; Periodic rekeying provides cryptographic self-healing to limit exposure from single key compromises; Formal verification using game-based proofs and symbolic evaluation confirms PQ3 secrecy properties against quantum adversaries
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Level 3 security; ML-KEM (Kyber)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid design combining post-quantum algorithms with Elliptic Curve algorithms; backward-compatible rekeying frequency updates
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Post-quantum key has significantly larger wire size than existing protocol; periodic rekeying used to amortize message size and avoid excessive overhead
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: iOS 17.4 or later; iPadOS 17.4 or later; macOS 14.4 or later; watchOS 10.4 or later
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Threats, Hybrid-crypto, Migration-program, Crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: formal verification, symbolic evaluation, game-based proofs, reductionist security methodology, machine-checked proofs using Tamarin prover, independent assessment of source code
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: phased rollout, backward-compatible updates, automatic ramping up to post-quantum encryption protocol, replacement of existing protocol within supported conversations
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected

## Entrust KeyControl

- **Reference ID**: Entrust KeyControl
- **Title**: Entrust KeyControl
- **Authors**: Key Management Systems (KMS)
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The document outlines a strategic roadmap for transitioning organizations from post-quantum preparedness to full PQC adoption using NIST standards and hybrid or pure migration approaches.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, HQC
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs), Harvest now decrypt later (HNDL) attacks
- **Migration Timeline Info**: NIST published first three PQC algorithms in August 2024; HQC draft standard expected in 2026; NSA favors exclusive PQC algorithm use by 2030 for National Security Systems
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States, France, Germany, European Union, United Kingdom; NIST, ANSSI, BSI, European Commission, NSA, NCSC
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Samantha Mabey (Director of Digital Security Solutions Marketing at Entrust); Jenn Markey (Advisor, Entrust Cybersecurity Institute)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Entrust KeyControl, nShield HSM, nShield 5 HSM v13.8.0
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS, IPsec, SSH, KMIP
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), Keys Secrets and Certificates Management, Cryptographic Security Platform
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, IETF, Open Quantum Safe, MITRE Post-Quantum Cryptography Coalition
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205, CAVP validation
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must move beyond PQ preparedness to implement PQC in production due to HNDL attacks and government mandates; PQC migration requires a staged enterprise-wide transformation with clear ownership rather than a simple switch; A hybrid approach offers operational continuity but adds complexity, while pure PQC ensures quantum readiness but risks legacy incompatibility; Enterprises should inventory all cryptographic assets and prioritize high-risk long-life data for migration; Systems must be built with crypto agility to allow swapping of cryptographic primitives as standards evolve.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid PQC migration combining traditional algorithms like RSA with PQC algorithms; Pure PQC migration replacing all traditional algorithms; Interim hybrid measures on the path to full adoption; Crypto agility to swap cryptographic primitives
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: CISO, Security Architect, Compliance Officer, Policy Maker, Developer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Unified cryptographic security platform; Complete inventory of cryptographic assets including algorithms protocols libraries keys and dependencies; Updated security policies including PQC adoption; Procurement policies embedding PQC requirements; Training for developers and security teams on PQC algorithms and key management
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Compliance, Migrate, Assess, Algorithms, Hybrid-crypto, Crypto-agility, Hsm-pqc, Migration-program, Pqc-governance
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: complete inventory of all cryptographic assets including algorithms, protocols, libraries, keys, and dependencies such as APIs and third-party integrations; unified cryptographic security platform to keep inventory current
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: controlled pilots in non-critical environments to validate PQC algorithm performance and interoperability; red teaming to simulate quantum breach scenarios and test incident response against HNDL attacks
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: quantum key distribution (QKD) mentioned as part of a hybrid approach in the European Commission roadmap
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: engage supply chain partners early on and regularly to confirm their roadmaps incorporate PQC; third party software bill of materials (SBOMs) for PQC
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: staged enterprise-wide transformation; phased rollout with Phase 1 for high-priority assets, Phase 2 for medium-priority assets, Phase 3 for remaining systems and archival data; hybrid vs pure migration approaches; IT refresh cycle alignment
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: assign PQC program ownership and accountability with authority to coordinate cross-functionally; train developers and security teams on PQC algorithms, key management, and migration pitfalls; commit to ongoing quantum resistance training and awareness across the organization

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: EJBCA 9.1+ or SignServer 6.0+; HSM model and firmware version supporting selected algorithms; Externalμ-ML-DSA support for large CRLs; CRMF or CMP for KEM key proof of possession

## AWS s2n-tls

- **Reference ID**: AWS s2n-tls
- **Title**: AWS s2n-tls
- **Authors**: TLS/SSL Implementation Software
- **Publication Date**: 2026-01-31
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: AWS has deployed ML-KEM hybrid post-quantum key agreement support to KMS, ACM, and Secrets Manager endpoints while phasing out CRYSTALS-Kyber by 2026.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, CRYSTALS-Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: harvest now, decrypt later threats
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Support for CRYSTALS-Kyber continues through 2025; removal across all AWS service endpoints in 2026 in favor of ML-KEM; deployment to all AWS services with HTTPS endpoints over the coming years.
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: aws partition, us-west-2 Region
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Alex Weibel: Senior Software Development Engineer in AWS Cryptography, contributor to s2n-tls, ACCP, and AWS-LC
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: s2n-tls, AWS Libcrypto (AWS-LC), rustls, AWS SDK for Rust, AWS SDK for Java v2, AwsCrtAsyncHttpClient, Amazon Corretto Crypto Provider (ACCP)
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, HTTPS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management Service, Certificate Manager, Secrets Manager, Cloud KMS
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 140-3
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDH, P256, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Customers must update TLS clients and SDKs to offer ML-KEM when connecting to AWS service HTTPS endpoints; Hybrid post-quantum TLS performance impact is negligible (0.05% TPS decrease) when using default SDK settings with connection reuse; CRYSTALS-Kyber support will be removed in 2026 requiring migration to ML-KEM; Enabling hybrid key agreement adds approximately 1600 bytes and 80–150 microseconds per handshake.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519MLKEM768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid post-quantum key agreement, ECDH+ML-KEM hybrid key agreement, graceful fallback to classical key agreement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 1600 additional bytes during TLS handshake; 80–150 microseconds more compute time; 2.3 percent TPS decrease without connection reuse; 0.05 percent TPS decrease with connection reuse
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: AWS SDK for Java v2 version 2.30.22 or greater; rustls package with prefer-post-quantum feature flag; AwsCrtAsyncHttpClient configuration with postQuantumTlsEnabled(true); Linux-based platforms for AWS Common Runtime HTTP client
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, hybrid-crypto, kms-pqc, migration-program, pqc-risk-management
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: FIPS 140-3 validation, benchmarks on example workloads, performance measurements using AWS SDK for Java v2
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: open-source FIPS-140-3-validated cryptographic library AWS Libcrypto (AWS-LC), open-source TLS implementation s2n-tls, dependency on AWS SDK for Java v2.30.22 or greater, dependency on rustls package with prefer-post-quantum feature flag
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: migration plan to deploy ML-KEM to all AWS services with HTTPS endpoints over coming years, phase out support for CRYSTALS-Kyber in 2026, graceful fallback to classical key agreement if clients do not upgrade by 2026, no code changes necessary for generally available release of AWS SDK for Java v2, TLS connection reuse amortizes handshake cost
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: customers must update TLS clients and SDKs to offer ML-KEM, security team or IT administrators may need to update firewalls to unblock new TLS algorithms
- **Extraction Note**: v3 update: 10 dimensions extracted; base fields from prior enrichment

## BoringSSL

- **Reference ID**: BoringSSL
- **Title**: BoringSSL
- **Authors**: TLS/SSL Implementation Software
- **Publication Date**: Continuous
- **Last Updated**: 2026-02-13
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Cloudflare's deployment of hybrid X25519+Kyber post-quantum key agreement for outbound connections to origin servers using TLS 1.3.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Kyber, ML-KEM, Kyber768, Kyber512, X25519Kyber768Draft00
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: store-now/decrypt-later threat
- **Migration Timeline Info**: October 2023: Free plan roll-out aiming for 100% by end of month; Pro and Business aiming for 100% by end of year; Enterprise start February 2024 with 100% by March 2024; Internal connections upgrade completion expected by end of 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Suleman Ahmad, Luke Valenta, Bas Westerbaan
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: BoringSSL, rustls, Chrome
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, TLS 1.2
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Reverse proxy, origin servers, load-balancer, middleboxes
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, AES, P-256, SHA
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid key agreement combining Kyber and X25519 ensures security even if one algorithm is broken; Split ClientHello packets caused by large post-quantum keyshares can break connections with buggy middleboxes; Using HelloRetryRequest allows gradual deployment by advertising support without sending large keyshares initially; Organizations should configure servers to prefer server ciphers and support TLS 1.3 for PQC adoption
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber512 targets AES-128 security level; Kyber768 targets AES-192 security level; X25519Kyber768Draft00
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key agreement (X25519+Kyber); HelloRetryRequest mechanism to avoid split ClientHello issues; GREASE mechanism for unknown codepoints
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: X25519 keyshare 32 bytes; Kyber768 client keyshare 1,184 bytes; Kyber768 server keyshare 1,088 bytes; X25519Kyber768Draft00 client keyshare 1,216 bytes; X25519Kyber768Draft00 server keyshare 1,120 bytes; Kyber768 client ops/sec 31,000; Kyber768 server ops/sec 70,000; X25519Kyber768Draft00 client ops/sec 11,000; X25519Kyber768Draft00 server ops/sec 14,000
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: TLS 1.3 support; BoringSSL library; nginx configuration with ssl_ecdh_curve X25519Kyber768Draft00:X25519; server cipher preference enabled; rustls version 0.21.7 or later for HelloRetryRequest support
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, hybrid-crypto, migration-program, pqc-risk-management, algorithms
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: fuzzing
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: third-party library trust, open-source vs proprietary

- **Main Topic**: Overview of post-quantum cryptography implementations in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0, including key exchange and signature algorithms available as Technology Preview.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, streamlined NTRU Prime
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs), harvest now decrypt later, steal store now decrypt later
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Migration to PQC will take years; RHEL 10.0 includes PQC as Technology Preview with staggered roll-out for General Availability anticipated as standards finalize
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Clemens Lang (author outlining implementations), Emily Fox and Simo Sorce (explained integration of PQC into Red Hat products)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: GnuTLS, OpenSSL, NSS, OpenSSH, libssh, oqsprovider, crypto-policies-pq-preview, crypto-policies-scripts
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, SSH, X.509, OCSP, Certificate Transparency
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Public key infrastructure (PKI), TLS certificates, software package signatures
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, IETF, Secure Shell Maintenance workgroup, LAMPS working group
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, elliptic curves, X25519, ECDH secp256r1, ECDH secp384r1, EdDSA, ECDSA, SHA-2, SHAKE256, AES-128, AES-192, AES-256
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC migration must start now to mitigate harvest now decrypt later attacks; RHEL 10.0 provides ML-KEM and ML-DSA as Technology Preview requiring manual policy configuration; Hybrid key exchange combines classic algorithms like X25519 with ML-KEM for TLS 1.3; SLH-DSA signatures are significantly larger and slower than ML-DSA but rely on hash functions; Future versions of RHEL will include PQC support in the DEFAULT crypto-policy
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512 (128 bit), ML-KEM-768 (192 bit), ML-KEM-1024 (256 bit), ML-DSA-44 (Category 1), ML-DSA-65 (Category 3), ML-DSA-87 (Category 5), SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange (X25519MLKEM768, SecP256r1MLKEM768, SecP384r1MLKEM1024), hybrid signatures combining ML-DSA with RSA, ECDSA, or EdDSA
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signature size 7856 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128f signature size 17088 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s signature size 16224 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-192f signature size 35664 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s signature size 29792 B; SLH-DSA-SHA2-256f signature size 49856 B; SLH-DSA takes orders of magnitude more processing time to generate and verify than ML-DSA
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Install crypto-policies-pq-preview and crypto-policies-scripts packages; switch system-wide cryptographic policy to DEFAULT:TEST-PQ; install oqsprovider package for ML-DSA in OpenSSL; use TLS 1.3 connections
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Threats, Migrate, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, pki-workshop

- **Reference ID**: Signal
- **Title**: Signal
- **Authors**: Secure Messaging and Communication
- **Publication Date**: 2026-02-20
- **Last Updated**: 2026-02-13

- **Main Topic**: Signal announces the PQXDH protocol upgrade combining X25519 and CRYSTALS-Kyber to provide post-quantum forward secrecy against future quantum threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: CRYSTALS-Kyber
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later; threat of a sufficiently powerful quantum computer breaking current public key cryptosystems
- **Migration Timeline Info**: PQXDH supported in latest client versions for new chats; X3DH to be disabled for new chats in coming months after sufficient update time; existing chats to be upgraded via software updates
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, X25519, elliptic curve cryptography
- **Key Takeaways**: Signal is implementing a hybrid approach combining classical and post-quantum algorithms to mitigate future quantum risks; The PQXDH protocol requires breaking both X25519 and CRYSTALS-Kyber to compromise shared secrets; Migration involves disabling legacy X3DH for new chats while rolling out updates for existing sessions; Quantum computers pose a Harvest Now, Decrypt Later threat even if not yet powerful enough to break current encryption
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key agreement combining elliptic curve cryptography and post-quantum key encapsulation mechanism; augmenting existing cryptosystems rather than replacing them
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Latest versions of Signal client applications; both sides of the chat must use latest Signal software for PQXDH activation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto, quantum-threats, Algorithms, Migrate, pqc-risk-management

## OpenSSH

- **Reference ID**: OpenSSH
- **Title**: OpenSSH
- **Authors**: SSH Implementation Software
- **Publication Date**: 2025-10-10
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: OpenSSH versions 9.0 through 10.1 support and default to post-quantum hybrid key exchange algorithms to mitigate store now, decrypt later attacks.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, sntrup761x25519-sha512, mlkem768x25519-sha256
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: store now, decrypt later; harvest now, decrypt later; cryptographically-relevant quantum computer
- **Migration Timeline Info**: OpenSSH 9.0 (April 2022) introduced PQC by default; OpenSSH 9.9 added mlkem768x25519-sha256; OpenSSH 10.0 (April 2025) made ML-KEM the default; OpenSSH 10.1 warns on non-PQC schemes
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OpenSSH
- **Protocols Covered**: SSH
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: None detected
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, x25519, SHA-512, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Update servers to OpenSSH 9.0+ for sntrup761x25519-sha512 or 9.9+ for mlkem768x25519-sha256; Hybrid algorithms ensure security even if post-quantum components are broken; Silence warnings selectively via WarnWeakCrypto only if accepting quantum risk; Signature algorithms like RSA and ECDSA face future risks but lack immediate store now, decrypt later urgency
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: mlkem768x25519-sha256; sntrup761x25519-sha512
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key agreement combining post-quantum and classical algorithms; WarnWeakCrypto option for managing non-PQC connections
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSH 9.0+ for sntrup761x25519-sha512; OpenSSH 9.9+ for mlkem768x25519-sha256; Check KexAlgorithms configuration; ssh_config(5) for WarnWeakCrypto
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: vpn-ssh-pqc, hybrid-crypto, quantum-threats, Algorithms, Migrate

## oqs-provider

- **Reference ID**: oqs-provider
- **Title**: oqs-provider
- **Authors**: Post-Quantum Cryptography Libraries
- **Publication Date**: 2025-07-29
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The oqs-provider is an OpenSSL 3.x plugin that bridges liboqs to enable NIST-standardized and candidate PQC algorithms for TLS 1.3, X.509 certificates, and key operations without code changes.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, BIKE, FrodoKEM, HQC, Classic McEliece, Falcon, MAYO, CROSS, UOV, SNOVA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: oqs-provider, liboqs, Open Quantum Safe project
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, X.509, CMS, CMP, PKCS#12
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, Key Management
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, ISO, IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, X25519, X448, SecP256r1, SecP384r1, SHA-2, SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: oqs-provider enables PQC TLS 1.3 and signature operations in OpenSSL 3.x without application code changes; Hybrid schemes combining classical and quantum-safe algorithms are supported for parallel use; Algorithms denoted with asterisks may not be enabled for TLS operations by default; OpenSSL 3.5+ disables oqs-provider implementations of standardized algorithms in favor of native support; Users must verify enabled algorithm subsets via openssl list commands due to build-time configurations.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87, SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-192s, SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-128s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-192s, SLH-DSA-SHAKE-256s
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid KEM schemes, hybrid signature algorithms combining classic and quantum-safe methods, composite algorithm IDs (e.g., p256_mlkem512, x25519_bikel1)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.0 or greater; liboqs installed; CMake tooling; specific OpenSSL versions for full TLS signature support (3.2+) and native PQC algorithm handling (3.5+); KEM encoding/decoding support for OpenSSL 3.4+ testing
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, pki-workshop, crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: algorithm enumeration via openssl list -signature-algorithms -provider oqsprovider and openssl list -kem-algorithms -provider oqsprovider; SBOM available in sbom.cdx.json
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: interoperability testing documented in ALGORITHMS.md; regression testing via scripts/runtests.sh; performance testing via openssl speed command (limited support in 3.0.2); conformance testing via KAT vectors implied by liboqs dependency

- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected

- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: hybrid KEM schemes combining classic and quantum-safe methods; backward compatibility with OpenSSL versions older than 3.5; breaking changes in OpenSSL 3.5 where oqsprovider disables ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA if native support exists; phased rollout via pre-build configuration to avoid default group limits (max 44 groups) in older OpenSSL versions

## Samsung S3SSE2A eSE

- **Reference ID**: Samsung S3SSE2A eSE
- **Title**: Samsung S3SSE2A eSE
- **Authors**: Hardware Security and Semiconductors
- **Publication Date**: 2026-01-06
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Samsung System LSI introduces S3SSE2A, the industry's first embedded Secure Element with dedicated hardware accelerators for post-quantum cryptography to protect mobile and IoT devices.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA (referenced as FIPS 204 module-lattice-based algorithm); Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now, decrypt later (HNDL) attacks; quantum computers decrypting existing security systems; side channel attacks; hardware reverse attacks; fault injection attacks
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Quantum computers expected commercially available after 2030; existing security systems could be rendered powerless as early as 2028; disruptive quantum threat likelihood of 33-54% in next 15 years
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: S3SSE2A; Thales secure OS; Thales PQC libraries; Active Shield; S-Laser
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Secure Element (eSE); Hardware accelerators; Non-volatile memory (NVM)
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Common Criteria EAL6+; Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) 203, 204, 205; Common Criteria (CC) EAL 4+
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA-2048
- **Key Takeaways**: Hardware PQC accelerators achieve approximately 17x faster computation than software-only implementations; Harvest now, decrypt later attacks necessitate immediate preventative action before quantum computers are commercially available; S3SSE2A provides independent security processing and storage separate from the application processor to mitigate side channel and reverse engineering attacks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: FIPS 204; ML_DSA65; EAL 6+; EAL 4+; 1M-qubit quantum computer capability by 2028; 160 hours decryption time for RSA-2048

## smallstep Certificate Authority

- **Reference ID**: smallstep Certificate Authority
- **Title**: smallstep Certificate Authority
- **Authors**: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Software
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Smallstep updated its certificate authority and CLI tools to support X25519Kyber768 hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange while noting current limitations of ML-DSA signatures.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now decrypt later attacks; Shor's Algorithm; quantum computers breaking current encryption schemes
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Updated on April 1, 2025; Go 1.24 update planned for August 2025 when Go 1.25 becomes available
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States (US Congress legislation); NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Richard Feynman (seminal 1981 talk on simulating physics with computers)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: step-ca; step-cli
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; X.509
- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI; Certificate Authority
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203; FIPS 204; FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519; RSA; ECDSA; EdDSA; ECDH; Diffie-Hellman
- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations should enable hybrid TLS handshakes using X25519Kyber768 to defend against harvest now decrypt later attacks; ML-DSA signatures are currently too large for practical certificate issuance; Hybrid certificates combining classical and post-quantum keys ensure compatibility during transition; Users must set GODEBUG=tlsmlkem=1 until Go 1.24 is updated in August 2025; AD CS users should explore alternatives as Microsoft is unlikely to add PQC support
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768; X25519Kyber768; 2048-bit RSA; 3nm node; 2nm node
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange; hybrid X.509 certificates; composite certificates with classical and post-quantum key pairs; gradual introduction of PQ algorithms before removing pre-quantum keys
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA signatures too large for practical use; ML-DSA public keys too large; 2048-bit RSA requires checking approximately 10^308 possible prime factors; Fugaku supercomputer needs millions of years to brute-force 2,048-bit RSA
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Developer; CISO; Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: step-ca v0.28.3+; step-cli v0.28.6+; GODEBUG=tlsmlkem=1 environment variable; TLS client support for X25519MLKEM768; Go 1.24 update planned for August 2025
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto; tls-basics; pki-workshop; migration-program; pqc-risk-management

## coreboot

- **Reference ID**: coreboot
- **Title**: coreboot
- **Authors**: Secure Boot and Firmware Security
- **Publication Date**: 2025-12
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The document discusses the critical role of quantum-safe secure boot in firmware initialization, highlighting algorithm choices, hybrid approaches, and supply chain challenges.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, ML-KEM, SLH-DSA, LMS, XMSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: 2025: First PQC use case for software and firmware signing mandated by CNSA 2.0
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; Europe; France; Germany
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Matthew Stubbs (Author)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PQMicroLib-Core, PQCryptoLib-Core, PQCryptoLib-SDK, PQPlatform-CoPro, PQPlatform-TrustSys, PQPerform-Flare, PQPerform-Inferno, PQPerform-Flex, PQCryptoLib-Embedded, PQPlatform-Hash, PQPlatform-Lattice, PQPlatform-SubSys, PQPerform
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Secure boot; Firmware; Semiconductor layer; Root-of-trust; Second-stage boot loaders
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: CNSA 2.0; FIPS 140-3 CMVP Level 3 or 4; FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, DSA, ECC
- **Key Takeaways**: Secure boot is foundational for cybersecurity and requires immediate action due to long device lifecycles; Hybrid PQ/T schemes are mandated by some governments but increase latency; Stateful signature schemes like LMS and XMSS pose risks for secure boot due to state management challenges; No end-to-end FIPS 140-3 certified PQC solutions currently exist, creating a bottleneck; Flexible hardware and software building blocks allow organizations to mitigate risk now while standards mature.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: PQ/T hybrid approach; Crypto agility; Double transition (hybrid to full PQC)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: PQ/T hybrid doubles latency compared to post-quantum verification alone; PQPerform offers high speed with larger area footprint; PQPlatform optimized for low area footprint.
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Policy Maker, System Integrator
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: FIPS 140-3 CMVP Level 3 or 4 certification (not yet available for PQC); Two-factor authentication mechanism; Hardware accelerators for optimization; State backup mechanisms for stateful signatures.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: crypto-agility, hybrid-crypto, code-signing, iot-ot-pqc, stateful-signatures
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: low memory footprint for an embedded device; low area footprint; power budget tradeoffs; flash/RAM limits implied by optimization for low memory and area
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: foundational semiconductor level of the supply chain; long development cycles and supply chains; devices built today could have a long shelf life; collaboration needed from various sources; bottleneck due to no end-to-end solution
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: PQ/T hybrid approach; double transition (transitioning into a PQ/T hybrid model then switching to full PQC); crypto agility enables systems to switch rapidly; context-specific solutions for closed systems vs interdependent systems; first-stage secure boot at semiconductor layer harder to implement than second-stage boot loaders
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected

## Linux IMA/EVM

- **Reference ID**: Linux IMA/EVM
- **Title**: Linux IMA/EVM
- **Authors**: Secure Boot and Firmware Security
- **Publication Date**: 2025-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Evaluation of integrating quantum-resistant signature algorithms (ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, HSS) into the Linux Integrity Measurement Architecture Appraise mechanism.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, HSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Advances in powerful quantum computers threatening classical asymmetric cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Bodies: National Institute of Standards and Technology, Internet Engineering Task Force; Regions: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Johannes Wiesböck, Maximiliane Münch, Michael Weiß
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Botan, Open Quantum Safe Project, hash-sigs, ima-evm-utils, KIWI
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Linux Integrity Measurement Architecture (IMA), Appraise mechanism, secure boot, file system extended attributes
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology, Internet Engineering Task Force
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: NIST FIPS 204, NIST FIPS 205, NIST SP 800-208, RFC 8391, RFC 8554
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, SHA2, SHAKE, elliptic curve signatures
- **Key Takeaways**: Lattice-based algorithms impose close to zero overhead or enhance performance compared to classical methods; Hash-based algorithms can introduce disproportionately high disk and performance overhead; File system mechanisms for storing extended attributes severely influence algorithm compatibility; Short SLH-DSA versions offer competitive performance while fast versions are not recommended due to increased boot times; HSS causes significantly longer boot times negatively impacting user experience
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-44, ML-DSA-65, ML-DSA-87, RSA-4096, SLH-DSA-SHA2, SLH-DSA-SHAKE
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Desktop boot time 23.0s to 24.0s with RSA-4096; Fastest boot times around 23.0s with Falcon and ML-DSA-44; HSS average boot time 35s; SHAKE-based SLH-DSA up to 1s slower than SHA2-based versions
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Linux kernel support for extended file attributes; File system compatibility (BTRFS, bcachefs, ext4); IMA policy configuration with appraise_type=imasig; Exclusion of /var/log from appraisal
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, stateful-signatures, code-signing, Assess, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: IMA Measurement List, runtime measurement list, file hashes in system audit logs
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Linux Test Project (LTP) test programs, ima_boot_aggregate, ima_measure, re-calculating boot aggregate, validating PCR aggregate value
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: Using IMA/EVM on Android, platforms without hardware TPM
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Open source implementations, linux-ima project page, ima-evm-utils package from source
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Kernel configuration, CONFIG_IMA, boot command line parameters (ima_policy=, ima_template=), recompiling kernel, creating trusted and EVM encrypted keys, building initramfs to load keys, mounting filesystems with iversion
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected

## BitLocker (Windows)

- **Reference ID**: BitLocker (Windows)
- **Title**: BitLocker (Windows)
- **Authors**: Disk and File Encryption Software
- **Publication Date**: 2024-10
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Microsoft BitLocker provides full disk encryption for Windows devices using AES-XTS, with key protection relying on classical RSA via TPM or alternative startup keys.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Trusted Platform Module (TPM); Microsoft Entra ID; Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS)
- **Standardization Bodies**: Trusted Computing Group (TCG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES-XTS; RSA; XTS-AES 128-bit
- **Key Takeaways**: BitLocker uses AES-XTS for encryption but relies on classical RSA for key protection via TPM; Device encryption automatically enables BitLocker on qualifying Windows devices starting with Windows 11 version 24H2; Recovery keys are backed up to Microsoft Entra ID, AD DS, or Microsoft accounts depending on device join status; Devices without TPM require a startup key on removable media or a password, though the latter is discouraged due to brute force vulnerability; System drives must be formatted as FAT32 for UEFI or NTFS for BIOS and remain unencrypted.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: XTS-AES 128-bit; TPM 1.2 or later; TPM 2.0 requires native UEFI mode
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: System drive recommended size approximately 350 MB; BitLocker free space requirement approximately 250 MB after activation
- **Target Audience**: IT professionals; Device administrators
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: TPM 1.2 or later; TCG-compliant BIOS or UEFI firmware; Secure Boot enabled; Native UEFI mode for TPM 2.0; NTFS formatted OS drive; FAT32 or NTFS system drive; mbr2gpt.exe tool for Legacy to UEFI conversion; BdeHdCfg.exe for volume creation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: compliance-strategy, migration-program, pqc-risk-management, data-asset-sensitivity, hsm-pqc
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: brute force attacks on password option without lockout logic; DMA access via externally accessible ports
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: migration from Legacy/CSM to native UEFI required for TPM 2.0; use mbr2gpt.exe tool before changing BIOS mode; decryption required before applying new encryption method or cipher strength; phased rollout via Windows versions (Windows 11, Windows 10, Windows Server 2016-2025)
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: intended for IT professionals and device administrators; requires Trusted Computing Group (TCG)-compliant BIOS or UEFI firmware; policy settings must be enabled for recovery key backup to Microsoft Entra ID or Active Directory Domain Services
- **Extraction Note**: v3 update: 10 dimensions extracted; base fields from prior enrichment

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Adversaries collecting encrypted content today with the hope of being able to decrypt them in the future
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Milestones: 2030 deprecation of existing algorithms; 2035 disallowance of existing algorithms; 1 January 2027 CNSA 2.0 required for new products; 31 December 2031 CNSA 2.0 mandated for all protocol use

- **Key Takeaways**: Sigstore has enabled ML-DSA for client-side signing experiments via BYOK; Public Sigstore services will wait for reliable PQCA in the Go ecosystem before adoption; CNSA 2.0 mandates PQC algorithms by 2031 for all protocol use; ML-DSA was chosen over SLH-DSA for initial support due to smaller signature size; Organizations must plan for verification material changes and potential dual-signature bundles during transition
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: cryptographic agility, Bring Your Own Key (BYOK), experimentation with ephemeral keys, potential dual-stack signatures in bundles
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: RSA public keys and signatures are 100s of bytes; elliptic curve keys and signatures are 10s of bytes; ML-DSA keys and signatures are in the kilobyte range; SLH-DSA keys are 10s of bytes; SLH-DSA signatures are 10 kilobyte range
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Compliance Officer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Go 1.25 for SLH-DSA support; Go 1.26 for ML-DSA support; sigstore-go signing Keypair interface implementation; cloud provider KMS offering support
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Timeline, Compliance, Migrate, Algorithms, crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: stateful hash-based signature schemes which makes safe implementation very difficult; signature stripping attacks

- **Reference ID**: Google Cloud KMS (Cloud Gateway)

- **Authors**: Cloud Encryption Gateways
- **Publication Date**: 2025-10
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Google Cloud KMS announces preview support for post-quantum Key Encapsulation Mechanisms including ML-KEM and X-Wing to protect against Harvest Now, Decrypt Later attacks.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, ML-DSA-65, SLH-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later; cryptographically-relevant quantum computer (CRQC)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Infrastructure upgrade to use post-quantum cryptography for connections expected fully available in 2026; HPKE support in Tink ready by end of this year
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Ioanna Tzialla (Software Engineer); Sophie Schmieg (Senior Staff Cryptography Engineer)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Cloud KMS, BoringCrypto, Tink, Marvell LiquidSecurity
- **Protocols Covered**: Hybrid Public Key Encryption (HPKE)
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cloud KMS, Cloud HSM
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, CNSA 2.0
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, P-256, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Developers must adopt high-level standards like HPKE to handle the architectural shift from traditional encryption to KEMs; Hybrid approaches combining classical and post-quantum algorithms are recommended to hedge against implementation flaws; Migration requires accounting for significantly larger key and ciphertext sizes impacting bandwidth and storage; Organizations should begin protecting sensitive data now to mitigate Harvest Now, Decrypt Later risks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768; ML-KEM-1024; ML-DSA-65; SLH-DSA; 1184 bytes key size for ML-KEM-768; 1568 bytes key size for ML-KEM-1024; 1088 bytes ciphertext for ML-KEM-768; 1568 bytes ciphertext for ML-KEM-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: X-Wing (combines X25519 and ML-KEM-768); Hybrid Public Key Encryption (HPKE)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-KEM-768 key is 18 times larger than P-256; ML-KEM-768 key size 1184 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 key size 1568 bytes; ML-KEM-768 ciphertext 1088 bytes; ML-KEM-1024 ciphertext 1568 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, CISO
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Deep cryptographic expertise for direct KEM use; adoption of HPKE standard via Tink library; account for larger payloads in resource-constrained environments
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: kms-pqc, hsm-pqc, hybrid-crypto, algorithms, threats
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs; Statement attestation with HSM
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: External Key Manager vendors: Equinix, Fortanix, Ionic, Thales, and Unbound; Google Cloud technology partners
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Key import from on-premises or external key management systems; 24-hour delay for key destruction; Automated and at-will key rotation; Multiple versions of a symmetric key active for decryption
- **Financial & Business Impact**: Cloud KMS: active key versions $0.06 per month; Cloud KMS: key use operations $0.03 per 10,000 operations; Cloud HSM: key versions (AES256, RSA2048) $1.00 per month; Cloud HSM: key versions (RSA 3072, RSA 4096) $2.50 per month (0–2,000), $1.00 per month (2,001+); Cloud HSM: key versions (EC P256, EC P384) $2.50 per month (0–2,000), $1.00 per month (2,001+); Cloud EKM: key versions $3.00 per month; Cloud EKM: key use operations $0.03 per 10,000 operations
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: X25519MLKEM768
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest now, decrypt later (HNDL)

- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Christopher van de Sande (Author); Konstantinos Karagiannis (Argued at DEF CON 33)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: NGINX; OpenSSL; liboqs; oqs-provider; F5 NGINX Ingress Controller; F5 NGINX Gateway Fabric

- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; NSA; CISA
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519; prime256v1
- **Key Takeaways**: Not all Linux distributions ship with OpenSSL 3.5 required for default PQC support; Users on older OpenSSL versions must install liboqs and oqs-provider to enable PQC; OpenSSL 3.0.x supports PQC key exchange but not server authentication signatures; Hybrid cipher suites combining X25519MLKEM768 with classical curves are recommended for immediate deployment
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: X25519MLKEM768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid PQC cipher (X25519MLKEM768); Dual-stack curve configuration (PQC + classical curves)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.5+ or liboqs and oqs-provider installed; NGINX compiled with compatible OpenSSL version; OPENSSL_CONF environment variable set for OQS provider; build tools (cmake, ninja-build, git) for manual compilation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics; hybrid-crypto; migration-program; pqc-risk-management; openssl-studio
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: OpenSSL version verification via nginx -V command; PQC-safe cipher validation using openssl s_client CLI test
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: OpenSSL CLI test with hybrid PQC cipher groups (X25519MLKEM768); NGINX configuration syntax test (nginx -t) with OQS-Provider environment variable
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: ARM based machines requiring architecture replacement from x86_64 to aarch64 in OQS-Provider module path
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Dependency on OpenSSL 3.5.x or liboqs/oqs-provider projects; reliance on Linux distribution OpenSSL versions (Debian, Alpine, Ubuntu, RHEL); F5 NGINX Ingress Controller and Gateway Fabric image dependencies
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Phased rollout based on Linux distribution availability; workaround required for OpenSSL 3.0.x via liboqs installation; limitation of PQC signature algorithms in TLS 1.3 server authentication until OpenSSL 3.2+; Helm chart image tag updates for Kubernetes deployments
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: Requirement to update Linux distributions or manually compile liboqs and oqs-provider; need to set OPENSSL_CONF environment variable in Systemd, Docker, or Kubernetes management tools

- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid classical+PQC key exchange, crypto-agility, composite certificates (X.509 RSA and ECC with ML-KEM, ML-DSA), custom cipher support via CSDK
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Up to 1 Gbps encrypted throughput; CN7108 weight 420g; CN7107 weight 1.9kg; CN7105 weight 5.8kg; Operating temperature -40°C to 85°C
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Operations, Compliance Officer, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Senetas CM7 Network Manager (CM7); FIPS 140-3 Level 1 certified module; Cryptographic SDK (CSDK) for custom ciphers; External Key Servers (CipherTrust) optional
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: crypto-agility, hybrid-crypto, qkd, iot-ot-pqc, compliance
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: Central configuration and management with Senetas CM7 Network Manager (CM7); Supports Syslog, NTP, SNMPv1 read only monitoring; Alarm, event and audit logs
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: FIPS 140-3 Level 1 certified; MIL-STD-810G compliance for shock and vibration
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: Hybrid QKD+PQC support
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: NIST-validated key generation
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: Fanless rugged enclosures; SWAP optimized for Size, Weight and Power in-vehicle and mobile deployments; Operating: -40°C to 85°C; Humidity: 5% – 95% non-condensing; Weight: 420g (14.81oz); Dimensions: 132.8mm (W) x 100mm (D) x 34.8mm (H)
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Subsidiary of Thales; Distributed exclusively by Thales outside Australia & New Zealand; Toolkit for Custom Cipher Development (CSDK): supports sovereign and custom algorithms
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: Crypto Agility; Platform Agility enabling flexible deployments across IT and OT environments; Network Agility protecting voice, video, and data across any network; Supports unicast, multicast and broadcast traffic; Seamless connectivity for any topology; Quantum resistant hybrid encryption using latest ML-KEM, ML-DSA standards

- **Key Takeaways**: ML-DSA and hybrid schemes are experimental and should not be used in production; AES-GCM key rotation is recommended before 2^32 encryptions per NIST SP 800-38D; Convergent encryption version 3 resists offline plaintext-confirmation attacks; Vault does not store data sent to the transit engine, shifting storage responsibility to the caller; FIPS 140-3 mode excludes ChaCha20-Poly1305 and Ed25519.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 128-bit AES key, 256-bit AES key, 2048-bit RSA, 3072-bit RSA, 4096-bit RSA, 256-bit ChaCha20-Poly1305 key, 96-bit nonce
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid combination of two signature algorithms (experimental)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Vault HTTP API maximum request size 32MB; AES-GCM rotation limit approximately 2^32 encryptions per key version
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Base64 encoding of plaintext data; Vault token with proper permissions; HSM support for PKCS#11 if importing keys
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, kms-pqc, hsm-pqc, crypto-agility
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: nonce reuse risk in convergent encryption version 1; offline plaintext-confirmation attacks in convergent encryption version 2; denial of service via maximum request size limits
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: key versioning and working set management; archive storage for older key versions; algorithm enumeration via supported key types list

- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: rewrap operation to upgrade encrypted data without revealing plaintext; key rotation guidance based on NIST publication 800-38D; convergent encryption version upgrades via rotate operation; backward compatibility via key rings for decryption of old versions
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: operator responsibility for encryption/decryption burden; ACLs to restrict key management vs application access; experimental features not recommended for production environments

## Element (Matrix Protocol)

- **Reference ID**: Element (Matrix Protocol)
- **Title**: Element (Matrix Protocol)
- **Authors**: Secure Messaging and Communication
- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Analysis of reported cryptographic issues in the vodozemac library and defense of Matrix's authenticated key distribution model against alleged vulnerabilities.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Andrew Morgan (anoa); dkasak; Gnuxie; Soatok; Trevor Perrin
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: vodozemac
- **Protocols Covered**: Olm, Megolm, ECIES, EdDSA, RFC 8628 Device Authorization Grant
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, Ed25519, Olm, Megolom, DH
- **Key Takeaways**: Matrix's authenticated key distribution model prevents network attackers from substituting low-order public keys to force predictable session keys; The vodozemac library does not currently reject all-zero X25519 outputs but this is deemed non-exploitable in the current Matrix context; Truncated 64-bit message authentication tags are a known trade-off common in deployed messaging systems like Signal; Downgrade attacks from experimental Olm V2 to V1 are not a current concern as only V1 is standardized and deployed; The check code in ECIES is a UX feature requiring user engagement rather than a cryptographic security check.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 64-bit message authentication tags; MAX_MESSAGE_KEYS constant of 40; MAX_MESSAGE_GAP hard limit of 2000
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: MAX_MESSAGE_KEYS = 40; MAX_MESSAGE_GAP = 2000
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: vodozemac library; Rust implementation of Olm and Megolm; Ed25519 identity key signatures for device authentication
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, crypto-agility, tls-basics, code-signing, api-security-jwt
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: nonce reuse, memory safety failures

- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-18
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: This document specifies the eXtended Merkle Signature Scheme (XMSS), WOTS+, and XMSS^MT as hash-based digital signature systems resistant to quantum computer attacks.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: XMSS, XMSS^MT, WOTS+
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Attacks using quantum computers; resistance against quantum-computer-aided attacks
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Andreas Huelsing; Denis Butin; Stefan-Lukas Gazdag; Joost Rijneveld; Aziz Mohaisen

- **Standardization Bodies**: Internet Research Task Force (IRTF); Crypto Forum Research Group (CFRG)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA-2; SHAKE
- **Key Takeaways**: XMSS provides security relying only on cryptographic hash function properties without requiring collision resistance; Hash-based signatures are naturally resistant to side-channel attacks and withstand known quantum computer attacks; The document defines both single-tree (XMSS) and multi-tree (XMSS^MT) variants to address slow key generation; WOTS+ is specified as the one-time signature building block for XMSS schemes.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Small private and public keys; fast signature generation and verification; large signatures; relatively slow key generation

## FreeBSD

- **Reference ID**: FreeBSD
- **Title**: FreeBSD
- **Authors**: Operating Systems
- **Publication Date**: 2024-12
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: FreeBSD 15.0 introduces native Post-Quantum Cryptography support via OpenSSL 3.5 and OpenSSH 10, alongside pkgbase system management and ZFS 2.4 features.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, sntrup761
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: FreeBSD 15.0 released December 2, 2025; Heimdal removal scheduled for FreeBSD 16
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Josphat Mutai
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OpenSSL 3.5, OpenSSH 10, FreeBSD 15.0
- **Protocols Covered**: SSH, TLS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, X448, SecP384r1, SecP256r1, curve25519-sha256, ecdh-sha2-nistp256, ecdh-sha2-nistp384, ecdh-sha2-nistp521, Ed25519
- **Key Takeaways**: FreeBSD 15.0 enables quantum-resistant TLS and SSH by default without configuration changes; Hybrid algorithms like X25519MLKEM768 provide belt-and-suspenders security during the transition period; ML-DSA signatures require significantly larger key sizes compared to classical Ed25519; OpenSSH 10 includes automatic keystroke timing obfuscation to prevent side-channel attacks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM-1024, ML-DSA-65, mlkem768x25519-sha256, sntrup761x25519-sha512
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid algorithms combining classical and post-quantum key exchange; Fallback chain to classical algorithms for compatibility with older clients
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA-65 private key 5.6 KB; ML-DSA-65 public key 2.7 KB; Ed25519 key 64 bytes; ZFS block cloning ratio 2.00x
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.5+; OpenSSH 10+; FreeBSD 15.0-RELEASE amd64; Enable pkgbase repository via /usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/FreeBSD-base.conf
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, tls-basics, OpenSSL Studio
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: keystroke timing leakage mitigated via automatic padding in OpenSSH 10; TOCTOU race condition in jail operations resolved via file descriptor-based operations
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: algorithm enumeration via `openssl list -kem-algorithms`; base system package inventory via `pkg query -a '%n' | grep '^FreeBSD-'`
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: signature verification using `openssl pkeyutl -verify`; version confirmation via `openssl version`, `zfs version`, and `ssh -V`
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: 32-bit platform retirement (i386, armv6, 32-bit PowerPC dropped); service jails for minimal daemon isolation; USB HID driver support for game controllers and digitizers
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: base system split into 310 individual packages managed via `pkg`; MIT Kerberos replaces Heimdal to improve interoperability; OpenSSL 3.5.4 LTS included in base
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: breaking changes include retirement of `freebsd-update` and 32-bit platforms; backward compatibility maintained for older SSH clients via fallback chain; migration from FreeBSD 14 required for i386 servers; Heimdal removal scheduled for FreeBSD 16
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: quantum-safe by default configuration requiring no opt-in for OpenSSH 10; granular base management via `pkg` replacing monolithic updates
- **Extraction Note**: v3 update: 10 dimensions extracted; base fields from prior enrichment
- **Source Document**: FreeBSD.html (98,609 bytes, 15,000 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-16T10:02:09

## ChromeOS

- **Reference ID**: ChromeOS
- **Title**: ChromeOS
- **Authors**: Operating Systems
- **Publication Date**: 2025-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-04
- **Document Status**: Partially Verified
- **Main Topic**: Google Chrome is switching to ML-KEM hybrid TLS 1.3 key exchange in version 131, while NIST finalizes PQC standards and a side-channel flaw affects Infineon-based YubiKey devices.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA, XMSS
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Chrome version 131 release in early November 2024; FN-DSA finalization later this year
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: David Adrian, David Benjamin, Bob Beck, Devon O'Brien (Chrome Team); Thomas Roche (NinjaLab); Victor Lomne
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Chrome web browser, SymCrypt, BoringSSL
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, FIDO
- **Infrastructure Layers**: HSM, security microcontrollers, firmware signing
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: Common Criteria
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, ECDSA, RSA, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Google is abandoning KYBER for ML-KEM due to incompatibility in final versions; Hybrid post-quantum key exchange codepoints are changing from 0x6399 to 0x11EC; Infineon microcontrollers have a side-channel flaw allowing ECDSA private key extraction; Vulnerable YubiKey devices cannot be patched and must be replaced or deprecated; Transition to PQC is a complex, multi-year iterative process requiring careful planning
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, ML-KEM768+X25519, Kyber768+X25519
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid ML-KEM, hybrid post-quantum key exchange, composite certificates (implied by hybrid approach), crypto agility (implied by flag/policy)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: None detected
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, CISO, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Chrome version 131; PostQuantumKeyAgreementEnabled flag; enterprise policy configuration; YubiKey firmware f5.7 or YubiHSM 2.4 for Infineon library deprecation
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, migration-program, pqc-risk-management
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: electromagnetic side-channel, physical possession of secure element, EUCLEAK (CVE-2024-45678)
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Common Criteria certification evaluations
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: security microcontrollers, YubiKey 5 Series, YubiKey 5 FIPS Series, YubiKey 5 CSPN Series, YubiKey Bio Series, Security Key Series, YubiHSM 2, YubiHSM 2 FIPS
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Infineon SLE78, Optiga Trust M, Optiga TPM, third-party cryptographic library dependency, deprecation of vendor library in favor of own library
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: breaking changes between Kyber and ML-KEM, incompatible hybrid post-quantum key exchange approaches, multi-year iterative process, firmware version updates (YubiKey f5.7, YubiHSM 2.4), inability to update existing vulnerable devices
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: complex planning required for transition to post-quantum cryptography
