- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-04
- **Document Status**: Partially Verified
- **Main Topic**: AWS announces the general availability of ML-DSA integration into AWS KMS for creating post-quantum digital signatures with three security levels.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptographically relevant quantum computers; emerging threats to public-key cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: US West (N. California); Europe (Milan)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jake Massimo; Mayank Ambaliya; Panos Kampanakis
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: AWS KMS; OpenSSL 3.5
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3; X.509; JWT; RFC7519
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Key Management; HSM; Cloud KMS
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 204; FIPS 202; FIPS-140-3 Level 3
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDSA; SHAKE256; HMAC-SHA256
- **Key Takeaways**: AWS KMS now supports ML-DSA key creation and signing operations in US West (N. California) and Europe (Milan); Messages larger than 4096 bytes require external pre-digesting using the EXTERNAL_MU message type; Three ML-DSA key specs (ML_DSA_44, ML_DSA_65, ML_DSA_87) offer NIST security levels 1, 3, and 5 respectively; Post-quantum signatures are critical for long-lived roots of trust in firmware and embedded devices; Local signature verification is possible using OpenSSL 3.5 with public keys retrieved from AWS KMS
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST Level 1 (ML_DSA_44); NIST Level 3 (ML_DSA_65); NIST Level 5 (ML_DSA_87); ML_DSA_SHAKE_256; 128-bit security; 192-bit security; 256-bit security
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML_DSA_44 Public key 1312 bytes; ML_DSA_44 Private key 2560 bytes; ML_DSA_44 Signature 2420 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Public key 1952 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Private key 4032 bytes; ML_DSA_65 Signature 3309 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Public key 2592 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Private key 4896 bytes; ML_DSA_87 Signature 4627 bytes; RAW message limit 4096 bytes; External mu digest size 64 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.5; AWS CLI; FIPS-140-3 Level 3 certified HSMs; pre-processing for messages larger than 4096 bytes
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: kms-pqc, hsm-pqc, code-signing, api-security-jwt, algorithms

- **Key Takeaways**: Do not rely on this experimental fork to protect sensitive data; use hybrid cryptography combining quantum-safe and traditional algorithms for deployment prior to standardization; test interoperability using this fork as a common third-party reference; wait for NIST standardization project outcomes before finalizing deployments.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: L1, L3, L5, bike-l1-sha512, bike-l3-sha512, bike-l5-sha512, classic-mceliece-348864-sha256, frodokem-640-aes-sha256, hqc-128-sha256, kyber-512-sha256, mlkem512-sha256, mlkem768-sha256, mlkem1024-sha384, ntruprime-sntrup761-sha512, dilithium2, dilithium3, dilithium5, falcon512, falcon1024, mayo1, mayo2, mayo3, mayo5, mldsa44, mldsa65, mldsa87, slhdsapuresha2128f, slhdsasha2128ssimple, slhdsashake128fsimple, slhdsashake128ssimple, slhdsasha2192fsimple, slhdsasha2192ssimple, slhdsashake192fsimple, slhdsashake192ssimple, slhdsapuresha2256f, slhdsasha2256ssimple, slhdsashake256fsimple, slhdsashake256ssimple
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange algorithms combining quantum-safe algorithms with traditional public key algorithms like RSA or elliptic curves; hybrid digital signature algorithms combining quantum-safe signatures with RSA3072 or ECDSA using NIST curves.
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: McEliece requires building oqs-openssh with a large stack provision (LDFLAGS="-Wl,--stack,20000000"); HQC algorithms have large stack usage and may cause failures on threads or in constrained environments.
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSH version 10.2; working C compiler; standard library and headers; libcrypto from LibreSSL or OpenSSL; zlib for transport compression; libfido2 for FIDO security token support; large stack provision (LDFLAGS="-Wl,--stack,20000000") for McEliece algorithms.
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, crypto-agility, migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: large stack usage causing failures on threads or in constrained environments; McEliece requires large stack provision (LDFLAGS="-Wl,--stack,20000000")
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: interoperability testing; regression testing; cygwin-based testing
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: constrained environments; large stack usage failures on threads; McEliece requires large stack provision
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: dependency on liboqs; fork of OpenSSH not endorsed by the OpenSSH project; reliance on NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography standardization project outcomes
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: experimental stage; not recommended for protecting sensitive data; hybrid cryptography combining quantum-safe with traditional algorithms (RSA, elliptic curves); interoperability testing against third-party implementations

- **Authors**: Network Operating Systems
- **Publication Date**: 2025-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Analysis of open standards and protocols requiring post-quantum algorithm integration or mitigation to address quantum computing risks.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, Streamlined NTRU Prime, ML-DSA, Stateful Hash-Based Signatures
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computing risk to cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Panos Kampanakis (AWS), Marc Manzano (SandboxAQ), Deirdre Connolly (SandboxAQ), Jayati Dev (Comcast)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OQS OpenSSH, WolfSSH, AWS Transfer Family, Tianocore EDK II
- **Protocols Covered**: SSH, MACsec, EAP-TLS, TLS 1.3, UEFI, PKCS #7, CMS, TCP, QUIC, FIDO2, DNSsec
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Firmware signing, Verified booting sequence, Certificate chain, Key distribution
- **Standardization Bodies**: IETF, IEEE, NIST, IANA, FIDO Alliance, ICANN
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, RFC9040, RFC9002, RFC6928, RFC6298, SP 1800-38C
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, SHA-512, SHA-1, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: SSH protocols currently lack an official IETF maintenance WG for PQ integration but rely on Expert Review codepoint assignments; Large PQC signatures in TLS over TCP and QUIC may exceed initial congestion windows causing extra round-trip times; UEFI specifications allow multiple signatures enabling hybrid classical and quantum-resistant firmware signing; FIDO2 faces challenges implementing hybrid algorithms on constrained devices like NFC tokens; DNSsec requires research into fragmentation due to large PQC key and signature sizes.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Quantum-resistant hybrid key exchanges, Multiple signatures for verifiers, Hybrid classical-PQ cryptographic algorithms
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA authentication data >15KB; TCP initial congestion window ~14KB (initwnd=10); QUIC Amplification window ~4KB; Packet pacing slowdown ~65ms per handshake; kInitialRtt default 333ms
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Researcher, Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: IANA Registry Expert Review codepoint assignments; Third-party open-source implementations for EDK II; Increased initial congestion window values; Packet pacing adjustments in QUIC
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, crypto-agility, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, digital-id, stateful-signatures, code-signing
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected

- **Reference ID**: Cisco Secure Client (AnyConnect)
- **Title**: Cisco Secure Client (AnyConnect)
- **Authors**: VPN and IPsec Software
- **Publication Date**: 2024-06
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The document outlines the transition to post-quantum cryptography, detailing quantum threats, algorithm comparisons, and the impact of hybrid implementations on network infrastructure like NGFWs.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium, Classic McEliece, XMSS, SPHINCS+, Rainbow, SIKE, Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC), harvest now decrypt later (HDNL), Shor's algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Cisco's quantum-safe roadmap targets 2027 for ML-KEM hybrid IKEv2 in Secure Client; Google Chrome enabled ML-KEM by default in May 2024
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Richard Feynman (suggested quantum systems simulation), David Deutsch (formalized theoretical model for quantum computers), Gurdeep Gill (Author)
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: Cisco Secure Client, Google Chrome
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, QUIC, IKEv2, IPsec, SSL
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs), VPN tunnels, deep packet inspection
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: CNSA 2.0
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: AES, RSA, ECC, SHA, Diffie-Hellman, DES, X25519
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid cryptography combines classical and post-quantum algorithms to provide dual-layer security against current and future threats; Larger key sizes in PQC implementations like ML-KEM can cause packet fragmentation and impact NGFW performance; Organizations must ensure cryptographic agility to seamlessly integrate new algorithms as quantum computing advances; The harvest now decrypt later risk necessitates immediate transition to PQC to protect sensitive data intercepted today.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024, ML-KEM-768 (implied via X25519MLKEM768 group identifier 4588)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid Key Exchange, X25519 + Kyber combinations, cryptographic agility
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: X25519 public key 32 bytes; X25519 ciphertext 32 bytes; Kyber-512 public key ~800 bytes; Kyber-512 ciphertext ~768 bytes; Kyber-768 public key 1,184 bytes; Kyber-768 ciphertext 1,088 bytes; Kyber-1024 public key 1,568 bytes; Kyber-1024 ciphertext 1,568 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-512 public key ~832 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-512 ciphertext ~800 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-768 public key 1,216 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-768 ciphertext 1,120 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-1024 public key 1,600 bytes; X25519 + Kyber-1024 ciphertext 1,600 bytes
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, CISO, Compliance Officer, Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Simultaneous client and headend (ASA/FTD) upgrade for Cisco Secure Client; NGFW firmware or software updates to support new cryptographic algorithms; hardware upgrades or optimizations for NGFWs to handle increased computational load
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Compliance, Migrate, Algorithms, hybrid-crypto, crypto-agility, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: PyCryptodome supports Hybrid Public Key Encryption (HPKE) alongside classical algorithms; The library offers accelerated AES on Intel platforms via AES-NI; Installation requires avoiding coexistence with the legacy PyCrypto library unless using the pycryptodomex package variant; Algorithms are implemented primarily in pure Python with C extensions for performance-critical components.

- **Key Takeaways**: Ledger Enterprise is not a custodian but provides technology for self-custody; The platform combines hardware, software, and firmware for end-to-end security; Organizations maintain full control of private keys with the ability to recover funds independently; The solution supports over 5000 coins and tokens across 15 blockchains; Ledger Enterprise is SOC 2 Type II certified and certified by ANSSI.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Supports 5000+ coins and tokens; Secures 15 blockchains; Represents more than 90% of global market capitalization of digital assets.
- **Target Audience**: CISO; Security Architect; Compliance Officer; Operations; Policy Maker
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: None detected
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: digital-assets; hsm-pqc; compliance-strategy; vendor-risk
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: side-channel attacks on fixed-key AES implementations; vulnerabilities in MPC implementation of fixed-key block ciphers; rogue transaction display attacks targeting endpoints; synchronous attacks on multiple unsecured endpoints
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: penetration testing; security certifications (Common Criteria, FIPS, CSPN); peer review of security proofs; standardisation initiatives assessment
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: true random number generators in certified secure hardware; entropy generation on secure hardware to prevent low entropy attacks
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: exponential complexity of MPC algorithms limiting signer count; computational intensity of key derivation (BIP-32) and TSS-based approval quorums; requirement for powerful hardware to overcome performance limitations
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: reliance on third-party security proofs that may be false after peer review; risk of rushing to deploy cutting-edge cryptography without understanding subtle details; exposure to operational risks from incomplete key lifecycle management in current MPC systems
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: MPC-only solutions not ready for production; need for hybrid approach combining MPC with secure execution environments and tamper-protected hardware; governance rules implementation outside MPC scope requiring hardened servers or HSMs; lack of trusted display in pure MPC schemes
- **Financial & Business Impact**: catastrophic consequences from implementing unready technology as security backbone; instantaneous and irrevocable loss of funds due to security breaches; operational risks for institutions using current MPC forms
- **Organizational Readiness**: need for secure execution environments for governance rules; requirement for dedicated secure hardware for key generation and storage; limitation of complex governance schemes in MPC-only solutions; necessity of regular business processes for key dissemination and revocation
- **Extraction Note**: v3 update: 10 dimensions extracted; base fields from prior enrichment

## Aptos

- **Reference ID**: Aptos
- **Title**: Aptos
- **Authors**: Blockchain & DLT Protocols
- **Publication Date**: Not specified
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-06
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Aptos proposes AIP-137 to introduce SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s as an optional post-quantum signature scheme prioritizing security over performance.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s, ML-DSA, Falcon
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Future quantum computing attacks; breaking 256-bit elliptic curve signatures; exposure of legacy address formats revealing public keys
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Plausible window for cracking 256-bit digital signatures by the mid-2030s; IBM plans 100,000-qubit chipsets by end of decade; PsiQuantum targets one million photonic qubits by end of decade
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: None detected
- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Validators, indexers, wallets, developer tools
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 205
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: Ed25519, SHA-256, 256-bit elliptic curve signatures
- **Key Takeaways**: Aptos prioritizes conservative hash-based security over raw performance to avoid untested mathematical assumptions; SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s offers robustness with significant signature size and verification speed overheads compared to Ed25519; The rollout is optional and phased via feature flags, allowing selective adoption without forced migration; Industry estimates suggest a threat horizon for breaking 256-bit signatures by the mid-2030s requiring proactive preparation
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s; 48-byte secret keys; 32-byte public keys; 7,856 bytes signature size; 294 microseconds verification time; 256-bit elliptic curve signatures
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Optional activation layer alongside default Ed25519; phased rollout via feature flags; selective adoption by users
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Signatures measure 7,856 bytes (82 times larger than Ed25519); verification takes approximately 294 microseconds (4.8 times slower than Ed25519); Falcon signatures around 1.5 KB
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Policy Maker, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Feature flags to coordinate rollout; infrastructure adjustments for validators, indexers, wallets, and developer tools
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: digital-assets, stateful-signatures, migration-program, pqc-risk-management, algorithms
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: floating-point arithmetic error-proneness in Falcon implementations; memory safety failures avoided by using hash-based schemes over exotic mathematics
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: None detected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected

- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: phased rollout strategy; optional activation via feature flags; backward compatibility maintained with Ed25519 as default; no forced migration; controlled rollout across validators, indexers, wallets, and developer tools
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: on-chain governance required for activation; ecosystem participants need time to adjust infrastructure; conservative preparation timeline before mid-2030s threat horizon

- **Key Takeaways**: Sui is designed for cryptographic agility to adapt to quantum-resistant cryptography; NIST mandates deprecation of classical algorithms by 2030 and full transition by 2035; High-value data requiring long-term privacy must be encrypted with PQ algorithms immediately due to farming attacks; Lattice-based schemes like Dilithium and FALCON are preferred over SPHINCS+ for Sui's high-throughput needs; Batch verification optimizations for PQ signatures currently lag behind ECC capabilities

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: ML-DSA signature 2420 B at 128 bits; Falcon signature 666 B at 128 bits; SPHINCS+ signature 7856 B at 128 bits; Dilithium batch verification speedup 20 to 50 percent; ML-KEM ciphertext 768 B at 128 bits

- **Key Takeaways**: BasejumpQDN extends quantum key exchange networks beyond the natural ~300 km limit using trusted repeater nodes; The solution is vendor-neutral, preventing lock-in by supporting hardware from ID Quantique, Toshiba, and others; BasejumpSIM allows cloud-based simulation of QDN deployments to model capacity and failure scenarios before physical rollout; Intelligent key routing optimizes network availability and reduces hardware costs by minimizing paired devices; Future upgrades to zero-trust quantum repeater nodes will reduce costs by eliminating the need for physical security or certification.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ~300 km limit for quantum links over optical fibre
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: live hybrid deployment with Nokia and ID Quantique for Proximus; seamless upgrade path from trusted repeater nodes to zero-trust quantum repeater nodes
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Quantum links limited to a few hundred kilometers; BasejumpQDN extends range beyond ~300 km limit
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; CISO; Operations; Policy Maker

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Kyber, Dilithium
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: 2025: ACME model stops being sufficient; 2025-2027: companies discover distributed keys and certificates requiring adaptation

- **Infrastructure Layers**: PKI, DevOps pipeline, K8s controllers, HSM, edge devices, CDN, load balancers, IoT, MQTT, API gateway
- **Standardization Bodies**: CA/B Forum, NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: PCI DSS
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDSA, RSA 2048, SHA-1, SHA-256
- **Key Takeaways**: ACME must evolve to handle hybrid certificates combining classical and post-quantum algorithms; Current ACME clients are incompatible with PQC due to library limitations and payload size constraints; Hybrid CSR generation requires new validation processes for SPKI structures and algorithm combinations; Organizations face significant challenges in automating key rotation across distributed infrastructure including edge and IoT devices
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Kyber 768, RSA 2048
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid TLS, hybrid certificates, composite CSR merging classical and post-quantum algorithms, dual-key generation (ECDSA and PQC)
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Kyber keys are an order of magnitude larger than ECDSA; Hybrid SPKI is several times heavier than classical structures; CSR size exceeds previous boundaries; Larger handshake packets trigger buffer issues on IoT devices
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, DevOps Engineer, Compliance Officer
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3.x; updated cryptographic libraries; modernized DevOps pipelines; compatible K8s controllers; HSM resources supporting PQC; updated edge devices
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto, tls-basics, migration-program, pqc-risk-management, iot-ot-pqc

- **Testing & Validation Methods**: conformance testing, interoperability testing, regression testing
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: embedded viability, IoT devices having insufficient buffer sizes, legacy load balancers treating large ClientHello as suspicious, WAF systems blocking PQC handshakes as anomalies, CDNs needing to support larger key footprints
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: dependency chains, third-party library trust, open-source vs proprietary, vendor PQC roadmap maturity
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: migration phase (assess/plan/test/migrate/launch), breaking changes, backward compatibility, rollback procedures, effort estimation, phased rollout, renewal errors, failing handshakes, environment inconsistencies, incompatible certificates, CDN region issues, IoT and edge incidents, non-compliance with CA/B Forum policies

## pqc-flow

- **Reference ID**: pqc-flow
- **Title**: pqc-flow
- **Authors**: Network Testing & Validation Tools
- **Publication Date**: 2024-01-01
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-21
- **Document Status**: Partially Verified
- **Main Topic**: pqc-flow is a production-ready passive network flow analyzer that detects Post-Quantum Cryptography and hybrid algorithm usage in live SSH, TLS, QUIC, and IKE traffic without storing payloads.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, sntrup761x25519-sha512@openssh.com, kyber, ntruprime, X25519Kyber768, X25519Kyber1024, X25519+ML-KEM-768, P-256+ML-KEM-768
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers will break current cryptography
- **Migration Timeline Info**: NIST standardized PQC in 2024; OpenSSH 9.0+ (2022) includes sntrup761x25519 hybrid key exchange by default; CISA, NSA, NIST mandate PQC migration timelines for federal systems
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: Bodies: CISA, NSA, NIST
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: None detected
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: pqc-flow, nDPI, libpcap, OpenSSH, Chrome, Firefox, Cloudflare, AWS

- **Infrastructure Layers**: Network flow analysis, packet capture, handshake parsing
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, curve25519-sha256, secp256r1 (P-256), P-256
- **Key Takeaways**: Passive flow analysis enables PQC detection without payload retention for privacy compliance; Hybrid algorithms like sntrup761x25519 and X25519Kyber768 are already deployed in production environments; Organizations can monitor quantum readiness posture by parsing SSH KEXINIT and TLS supported_groups extensions; Detection covers both live traffic via AF_PACKET and offline PCAP analysis; Custom parsers are required to identify specific PQC group IDs like 0x11ec and 0x2001
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768, X25519Kyber768, X25519Kyber1024, X25519+ML-KEM-768, P-256+ML-KEM-768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: sntrup761x25519-sha512@openssh.com, X25519Kyber768, X25519+ML-KEM-768, P-256+ML-KEM-768
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Bounded memory per flow ~24KB during handshake; Handshake metadata <6KB per flow; Sub-100ms export latency in live mode
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: CMake 3.16+; libpcap-dev; nDPI 4.11+; AF_PACKET TPACKET_V3 support; sudo or cap_net_raw/cap_net_admin capabilities

- **Main Topic**: PQC-LEO is an open-source framework for automating computational and network performance benchmarking of NIST PQC algorithms across x86 and ARM architectures using OpenSSL and OQS libraries.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, HQC
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected

- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: None detected
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC-LEO automates benchmarking of computational and network performance for PQC algorithms; The framework supports testing across x86 and ARM architectures using OpenSSL 3.6.1 and Liboqs; HQC algorithms are disabled by default due to specification non-conformance but can be enabled with user discretion; Memory profiling for Falcon variants is non-functional on ARM systems; Results are output as raw CSV files processed by Python scripts for analysis
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid-PQC, hybrid TLS
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Measures handshake latency; key generation time; signing/verification overhead; memory footprint; energy consumption; CPU usage metrics; peak memory usage metrics

- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Security strength up to 256 bits; Lifetime operating time greater than 25 years; Operating temperature range −40°C to 125°C

- **Implementation Attack Surface**: side-channel attacks on memory, secret leakage through countermeasures and attack detection schemes, traditional side-channel attacks, fault injection detection

- **Testing & Validation Methods**: NIST SP 800-22 randomness, NIST SP 800-90B entropy, voltage tests, temperature tests, device ageing tests, environmental operating data testing
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: quantum tunnelling current variations, oxide thickness variations, random distribution of defects (traps) in the gate oxide, high entropy seeds, NIST SP 800-90B entropy, True Random Number Generator (TRNG) IP
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: Embedded Linux, MPUs, IoT devices, standard CMOS process, Bulk CMOS, FDSOI, FinFET process technologies (55nm down to 12nm), secure element integration
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: risks associated with trusting third parties, supply chain security, reducing the cost and complication of HSMs, key injection on a factory floor

- **Financial & Business Impact**: reducing the cost and complication of HSMs

- **Key Takeaways**: Windscribe apps now support post-quantum WireGuard out of the box using hybrid key exchange; Users must log out and log back in to enable post-quantum encryption on existing sessions; The PresharedKey is rotated on each login to enhance security; Classical Curve25519 is vulnerable to quantum attacks, necessitating post-quantum protection; Hybrid X25519MLKEM768 mechanism secures the pre-shared key exchange against future quantum threats
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: hybrid key exchange mechanism X25519MLKEM768; post-quantum handshake on top of WireGuard

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest-now-decrypt-later; Shor's algorithm; Chen's algorithm; RSA breaking via quantum computers

- **Key Takeaways**: Quantum software optimizations by Craig Gidney have reduced the qubit requirement to break RSA-2048 from 20 million to under one million, accelerating Q-day estimates; Regulatory bodies globally are setting migration deadlines between 2030 and 2035 regardless of exact Q-day timing; Harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks pose an immediate threat as encrypted traffic can be harvested today for future decryption; Lattice-based cryptography remains the primary defense despite recent algorithmic scares like Chen's algorithm which was found to have a fundamental bug
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: RSA-2048; X25519MLKEM768; X25519Kyber768Draft00
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Post-quantum hybrid key agreement (X25519MLKEM768); Hybrid deployments using classical and post-quantum algorithms
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 20 million qubits (original estimate for RSA-2048); fewer than one million qubits (Gidney's optimized estimate); 242,000 superconducting qubits (lower bound estimate); 15 (largest number factored by quantum computer without cheating)

- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-30

- **Main Topic**: Akamai's implementation of TLS 1.3 hybrid key exchange using post-quantum cryptography to protect against harvest now, decrypt later threats.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, X25519MLKEM768
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: harvest now, decrypt later (HNDL)
- **Migration Timeline Info**: June 30, 2025: limited availability for Ghost to Origin; October 31, 2025: general availability for all origin TLS connections
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jan Schaumann, Chief Information Security Architect at Akamai, leads the internal Cryptography Group and launched the bug bounty program
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: OpenSSL, BoringSSL, nginx, tcpdump, Wireshark, Ghost to Origin (G2O)
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.3, TLS 1.2, HTTP
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cloud computing, Edge network, Origin servers, Client-side applications
- **Standardization Bodies**: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: X25519, RSA, ECDHE, DHE, AES256-GCM-SHA384, ECDSA
- **Key Takeaways**: Hybrid key exchange increases ClientHello size requiring trade-offs between bandwidth and round trips; Clients must configure TLS libraries carefully to advertise both classic and hybrid groups while sending only one key share initially; Akamai is rolling out PQC in phases starting with Ghost to Origin connections; OpenSSL and BoringSSL handle curve preferences differently affecting HelloRetryRequest behavior; Organizations should assess infrastructure for library updates to support crypto-agility.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: X25519MLKEM768, ML-KEM768, 32 bytes (X25519 public key), 256 bytes (RSA-2048 public key), 1,184 bytes (ML-KEM768 public key), 1,216 bytes (X25519MLKEM768 public key)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: TLS 1.3 hybrid key exchange, hybrid ECDHE-MLKEM key agreement, crypto-agile state, phased approach (Akamai-to-origin, Client-to-Akamai, Akamai-to-Akamai), HelloRetryRequest for group negotiation
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: X25519 public key 32 bytes; RSA-2048 public key 256 bytes; ML-KEM768 public key 1,184 bytes; X25519MLKEM768 public key 1,216 bytes; increased ClientHello size causes potential server rejection or bandwidth waste
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Operations, CISO
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: TLS library supporting TLS 1.3 hybrid key exchange; OpenSSL or BoringSSL updates; infrastructure assessment for library updates; understanding of SSL_CTX_set1_curves(3) or bssl client -curves options
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: tls-basics, hybrid-crypto, crypto-agility, migration-program, pqc-risk-management
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: crypto-agile state, identify necessary library updates, assess infrastructure
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: tcpdump, openssl s_client, Wireshark, packet analysis, validating a client, validating a server
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: OpenSSL, BoringSSL, third-party library trust, open source cloud, Akamai PQC provider
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: phased approach, Phase one: Akamai-to-origin, Phase two: Client-to-Akamai, Phase three: Akamai-to-Akamai, HelloRetryRequest, backward compatibility, limited availability, general availability release on October 31, 2025

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Falcon, NTRU-Prime
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Prohibiting classical cryptography after 2035 (NIST IR 8547); Australian ASD prohibits classical cryptography after 2030
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; Australia; NIST; Australian ASD
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Paul Miller: author of the @noble family of cryptographic libraries
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: noble-post-quantum, noble-hashes, noble-curves, jsbt

- **Key Takeaways**: NIST and Australian ASD mandate prohibiting classical cryptography by 2035 and 2030 respectively; Hybrid algorithms combining post-quantum and elliptic curve cryptography are recommended for key agreement and signatures; SLH-DSA is significantly slower than lattice-based alternatives but relies on conservative hash primitives; The library lacks constant-time protection against side-channel attacks in JavaScript environments

- **Publication Date**: 2025
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-31
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: AWS Transfer Family introduces post-quantum hybrid SFTP file transfers using ML-KEM and Kyber algorithms within SSH protocols.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: cryptanalytically relevant quantum computer (CRQC), harvest-now-decrypt-later
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States, Canada, Germany, France; U.S. Congress, White House, National Security Agency (NSA)
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Panos Kampanakis, Alex Volanis, Gerardo Ravago, Torben Hansen
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: AWS Transfer Family, OQS OpenSSH, wolfSSH, liboqs, AWS-LC
- **Protocols Covered**: SSH, SFTP, TLS 1.3
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Cloud KMS, Key Management Service (AWS KMS), Certificate Manager (ACM), Secrets Manager
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST, ISO/IEC, IEEE
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, FIPS 140, SP 800-56Cr2, CNSA 2.0
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: ECDH, P256, P384, P521, Curve25519, X25519, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, AES-192, ssh-ed25519
- **Key Takeaways**: AWS Transfer Family upgraded from Kyber to ML-KEM for quantum-resistant key exchanges; Hybrid SSH key exchange combines classical ECDH with post-quantum KEMs to protect against harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks; Users must update SFTP endpoints to TransferSecurityPolicy-2025-03 or TransferSecurityPolicy-FIPS-2025-03 for ML-KEM support; Interoperability is confirmed with OQS OpenSSH and wolfSSH clients using specific PQ-hybrid key exchange methods.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: mlkem768nistp256-sha256, mlkem1024nistp384-sha384, mlkem768x25519-sha256, Kyber-512, Kyber-768, Kyber-1024
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: PQ-hybrid key exchange combining classical ECDH with post-quantum KEMs; hybrid quantum-resistant key exchanges
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 1281 bytes for PQ-hybrid public key; 92-byte P384 public key; 1184-byte Kyber-768 public key; 5 bytes of padding; 1088-byte Kyber-768 ciphertext
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect, Developer, Compliance Officer, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Enable TransferSecurityPolicy-2025-03 or TransferSecurityPolicy-FIPS-2025-03 in SFTP endpoint; build OQS OpenSSH or wolfSSH with liboqs; use PEM-encoded or DER-encoded private keys for authentication
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: hybrid-crypto, vpn-ssh-pqc, algorithms, compliance, migrate
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: interoperability testing between Transfer Family and OQS OpenSSH/wolfSSH; packet captures using Wireshark to confirm negotiated key exchange methods; client output verification for algorithm negotiation
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: dependency on open-source libraries liboqs and wolfSSH; reliance on AWS-LC for FIPS compliance; use of OQS OpenSSH fork
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: migration from experimental Kyber policies to standardized ML-KEM policies (TransferSecurityPolicy-2025-03); requirement to update SFTP endpoints and client software; backward compatibility considerations for clients still using Kyber; phased rollout via security policy selection in AWS Management Console
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: alignment with Quantum Computing Cybersecurity Preparedness Act, NSM-8, NSM-10, and CNSA 2.0 requirements; need for FIPS compliance via specific security policies; preparation for harvest-now-decrypt-later threats

- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations can enforce post-quantum cryptography via predefined security policies without adding operational complexity; Migration to enhanced policies should start with BASIC mode and validate client behavior before enabling STRICT mode; API Gateway uses Server Name Indication (SNI) rather than HTTP Host headers to determine which security policy applies during TLS negotiation; Enhanced policies allow granular control over minimum TLS versions and cipher suites for regulated workloads.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected

- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: monitoring TLS usage and policy migrations using access logs variables $context.tlsVersion and $context.cipherSuite to identify legacy consumers and validate client readiness
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: validating client behavior using access logs and performance metrics in Amazon CloudWatch before enabling STRICT mode; confirming clients can connect with BASIC mode as expected
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: phased rollout starting with BASIC mode to validate client behavior and then moving to STRICT mode; rollback procedures available by reverting endpoint access mode back to BASIC; update propagation takes up to 15 minutes
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: aligning APIs with evolving security and compliance expectations such as PCI DSS, Open Banking, and FIPS; standardizing TLS configuration across API Gateway infrastructure without adding operational complexity

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Streamlined NTRU Prime, sntrup761
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Record now, decrypt later attacks
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected

- **Key Takeaways**: SimpleX Chat mitigates "record now, decrypt later" attacks by integrating Streamlined NTRU Prime into the double ratchet algorithm; Message size privacy is protected by padding all messages to a fixed block size rather than using space-efficient schemes like Padme; Full-stack repudiation (deniability) will be enabled in v5.7 by removing cryptographic signatures from client-client authorization; Break-in recovery is maintained by restricting profiles to a single device to prevent key rotation conflicts; End-to-end encryption security relies on two-factor key exchange via secondary channels to mitigate man-in-the-middle attacks
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid integration of Streamlined NTRU Prime with Signal double ratchet algorithm
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Messages padded to a fixed block size; Padme scheme deemed ineffective for mitigating message size attacks

- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-01
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: Integration and performance evaluation of five post-quantum signature algorithm families into CoreDNS to enable quantum-resistant DNSSEC functionality.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-DSA, FALCON, SPHINCS+, MAYO, SNOVA, HQC, CRYSTALS-KYBER, CRYSTALS-ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, Rainbow-Ia, RedGeMSS128, XMSS, SQISign
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Vulnerability of public-key cryptography based on number factorization or discrete logarithm to quantum attacks; threat to RSA and ECDSA algorithms
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Experts estimate quantum computer capability could occur in the next 10 years; NIST published ML-KEM, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA standards on August 13, 2024; HQC selection announced March 2025
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Julio Gento Suela; Javier Blanco-Romero; Florina Almenares Mendoza; Daniel Díaz-Sánchez; van Rijswijk-Deij; Müller; Shrishak; Shulman; Beernink; Rawat; Jhanwar; Schutijser; Goertzen
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: CoreDNS, BIND9, Unbound, PowerDNS, liboqs, OQS-BIND, PATAD testbed
- **Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC, DNS, UDP, TCP, TLS, HTTPS, gRPC
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 203, FIPS 204, FIPS 205, RFC 8624, RFC 4033, RFC 4034, RFC 4035, RFC 5702, RFC 7766
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA, ECDSA, ECC, ED25519, SHA-256, P256
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC algorithms introduce operational overhead with signature sizes up to 50 times larger than traditional algorithms; Falcon-512 is the only candidate identified that can be adopted without protocol modifications in some contexts; MAYO-2 outperforms RSA-1280 in signing performance while Falcon-512 performs comparably; Increased signature sizes often force the use of TCP transport, introducing additional latency; Hardware acceleration on x86-64-v3 architectures shows significant improvements for PQC algorithms compared to v2
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-KEM-768 (mentioned as standard), ML-DSA-44, Falcon-512, SPHINCS+-SHA-256-128s, MAYO-1, MAYO-2, SQISign-1, RSA-1280, 1,232-byte UDP limit
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Out-of-band key distribution; QNAME-based fragmentation (QBF); cipher-suite negotiation mechanisms; dual-stack deployment implied by TCP fallback requirement
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: PQC signatures and keys can be between 10 and 50 times larger than traditional algorithms; QBF resolves queries in 43±1ms compared to 83±1ms for standard DNS with TCP fallback; Unbound implementation demonstrated approximately 30% overhead
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Researcher, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: CoreDNS plugin architecture; Go programming language environment; Kubernetes clusters; x86-64-v3 hardware for optimal performance; liboqs integration for some implementations
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Migrate, Assess, Performance, Migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: None detected
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: benchmark, performance evaluation, empirical performance data, comparative analysis, controlled testing environments
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Shor's Algorithm; factoring integers; breaking RSA

- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Neal H. Walfield (author); Clemens Lang (Crypto Team Product Owner at Red Hat); Devan Carpenter, Justus Winter, Malte Meiboom (Sequoia contributors); Daiki Ueno, Jakub Jelen, Peter Vrabec, Roberto Hueso Gomez, Simo Sorce, Stanislav Zidek (Red Hat contributors)

- **Key Takeaways**: OpenPGP pairs post-quantum algorithms with traditional ones to mitigate risk if one is broken; SLH-DSA offers higher security confidence but larger artifacts and slower performance than ML-DSA; Organizations should use dual certificates for compatibility during the transition period; Post-quantum certificates require RFC 9580 format except for specific encryption subkeys; Implementation currently requires OpenSSL backend and pre-release software versions
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: ML-DSA-65 equivalent to AES-192; ML-DSA-87 equivalent to AES-256; SLH-DSA-128s equivalent to AES-128; SLH-DSA-256s equivalent to AES-256; ML-KEM-768 equivalent to AES-192; ML-KEM-1024 equivalent to AES-256
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid algorithms pairing PQC with classical (e.g., ML-DSA-65+Ed25519); Dual certificate strategy using one RSA and one PQC certificate for signing; Partial transition via subkeys in old format
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SLH-DSA signatures 8 kilobytes to 30 kilobytes; ML-DSA-65 signature 3.3 kilobytes; ML-DSA-87 signature 4.6 kilobytes; Ed25519 signature 64 bytes; Generated PQC certificate 42 kilobytes; Elliptical cryptography certificate 2.3 kilobytes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL backend; sequoia-openpgp version 2.2.0-pqc.1; sq version 1.4.0-pqc.1; RFC 9580 profile; cargo build with crypto-openssl feature
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms, Hybrid-crypto, Code-signing, Email-signing, Migration-program

- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: interoperability problems until standard is widely deployed; expected standard publication in first half of 2026; Debian release unlikely before July 2027; dual-certificate strategy with RSA and post-quantum keys; requirement for RFC 9580 profile for new certificate format; partial transition enabled by ML-KEM-768+X25519 in old format

- **Key Takeaways**: WinSCP supports hybrid key exchange methods combining lattice-based algorithms with classical elliptic curve methods; GSSAPI-based key exchange enables automatic credential re-delegation for long-running sessions; Data-based rekeys are critical to prevent 32-bit packet sequence number wraparound attacks; Users should avoid legacy 1024-bit Diffie-Hellman groups due to security warnings; Key exchange performance may impact connection startup time on slower machines.

- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Key exchange may take several tens of seconds on slow machines; Max data before rekey recommended at 1 gigabyte; Max time before rekey recommended at 60 minutes
- **Target Audience**: Developer, Security Architect, Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: SCP or SFTP file protocol selection; Kerberos V5 credentials for GSSAPI authentication

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA, FN-DSA
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer (CRQC), Harvest Now Decrypt Later (HNDL), Shor's algorithm, Grover's quantum algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: US federal government migration by 2035; UK inventory and plan by 2028 with full transition by 2035; Japan and Canada full transition by 2035; Australia transition by 2030; EU critical infrastructure by 2030 with full transition by 2035; TLS 1.3 support required by 2030
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States, UK, Japan, Canada, Australia, European Union, Netherlands

- **Key Takeaways**: Organizations must inventory cryptographic mechanisms vulnerable to quantum attacks immediately; Upgrading TLS and SSH key exchange functionality is the most urgent and easiest step to mitigate Harvest Now Decrypt Later risks; Migration plans should be broken into three parts: inventory, migrate TLS/SSH, and migrate everything else; PQC support requires adherence to NIST standards published in August 2024 or later
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: RSA-2048, AES-128, SHA-2, less than a million noisy qubits required to break RSA-2048
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid cryptography, quantum-safe hybrid cryptography
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 84% of accessible domains support TLS 1.3; 45% of total accessible domains support PQC key exchanges; largest number factored by quantum computer is 21 (or 15); Google Willow chip has 105 qubits

## PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC

- **Reference ID**: PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Title**: PowerDNS PQC DNSSEC
- **Authors**: DNS Security
- **Publication Date**: 2024
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-01
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: A field study evaluating the performance and deliverability of experimental Post-Quantum Cryptography algorithms in DNSSEC using PowerDNS and RIPE ATLAS.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: Falcon, Dilithium, SPHINCS+, XMSS, XMSSMT
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computers breaking current cryptography; Q-day
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Jason Goertzen; David Joseph; Peter Thomassen; Peter van Dijk
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: PowerDNS; liboqs; oqs-provider; Open Quantum Safe
- **Protocols Covered**: DNSSEC; DNS; TLS
- **Infrastructure Layers**: DNS Infrastructure; Authoritative Server; Recursor
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSASHA256; RSA
- **Key Takeaways**: PQC algorithms with larger signatures like SPHINCS+ and XMSS suffer significantly higher SERVFAIL rates due to packet size limits; Falcon demonstrates the fewest delivery issues due to its small signature size; Switching from UDP to TCP improves response success rates for large PQC signatures; Stateful hash-based signatures require careful parameter set selection based on zone size; Some resolvers incorrectly report DNSSEC validation (AD bit) for unsupported PQC algorithms.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: Falcon-512; Dilithium2; XMSS parameter sets; XMSSMT parameter sets
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: SERVFAIL rate for XMSS with NSEC3 (TCP, no DO bit) increases from 19.9% to 51.6%; Correct response rates for SPHINCS+ and XMSS increase from around 80% to 90% when switching from UDP to TCP; Dilithium2 correct response rate increases from 89.1% to 93.2% with TCP; Falcon correct response rate increases from 97.4% to 98.5% with TCP
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Operations
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: OpenSSL 3 provider; liboqs library; modified PowerDNS DNSSECKeeper; modified BIND 9
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; Playground; stateful-signatures; tls-basics
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: insecure reuse of state in stateful hash-based signatures; nonce reuse risks implied by state tracking requirements
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: interoperability testing across RIPE ATLAS network; performance impact evaluation; deliverability testing with varying transport (UDP/TCP) and DO bit settings; pre-filtering of probe-resolver combinations
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: None detected
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: None detected
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: dependency on Open Quantum Safe (liboqs); use of oqs-provider for OpenSSL 3; reliance on third-party libraries for PQC algorithm implementation
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: modification of BIND 9 and PowerDNS to support PQC algorithms; parameter set selection challenges for XMSS/XMSSMT based on zone size; SERVFAIL rate increases due to packet size limits; transition from classical to PQC signing schemes; dynamic signing vs presigned setup differences
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: None detected
- **Extraction Note**: v3 update: 10 dimensions extracted; base fields from prior enrichment
- **Source Document**: PowerDNS_PQC_DNSSEC.html (72,840 bytes, 13,709 extracted chars)
- **Extraction Timestamp**: 2026-04-16T14:30:07

- **Key Takeaways**: System owners should begin financial planning for PQC updates now; Operational systems should only use robust implementations of final standards like ML-KEM and ML-DSA; Hybrid schemes are recommended for interoperability during the transition phase; Stateful hash-based signatures require strict state management suitable only for specific use cases like firmware signing

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: SLH-DSA, ML-KEM
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Store Now, Decrypt Later; obsolescence of ECDSA due to increasing computing power
- **Migration Timeline Info**: Q1 2025: Quantum-secure PoW Mainnet live; Q3 2025: Certik Audit complete and Mainnet Launch; H1 2026: TGE & Exchange listings; H2 2027: Mainnet Upgrade for Post-quantum AI Communication Infrastructure

- **Key Takeaways**: HAETAE offers signature sizes 30% to 40% smaller than Dilithium at comparable security levels; Verification key sizes are reduced by 20% compared to Dilithium; The scheme uses bimodal distributions and hyperball uniform sampling to balance compactness and implementation simplicity; Targeted security levels correspond to NIST PQC levels 2, 3, and 5 with specific parameter sets; Implementation involves floating-point computations for sampling which introduces precision analysis challenges.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: NIST PQC security levels 2, 3, 5; HAETAE120 (Target security 120); HAETAE180 (Target security 180); HAETAE260 (Target security 260); q = 64513; Core-SVP classical hardness 120, 180, 260
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Signature size without rANS: 3040 bytes (HAETAE120), 4064 bytes (HAETAE180), 5792 bytes (HAETAE260); Expected signature size with rANS: 1473 bytes, 2268 bytes, 2737 bytes; Public key size: 1056 bytes, 1568 bytes, 2080 bytes; Signature sizes 30% to 40% smaller than Dilithium; Verification keys 20% smaller than Dilithium
- **Target Audience**: Researcher, Developer, Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Floating-point number support for real number computations; Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) with fully splitting polynomial ring; Prime modulus q = 64513; Polynomial ring R = Z[x]/(x256 + 1)

- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Quantum computer attacks on large integer mathematics; general quantum resistance of hash-based signatures
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected

- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: None detected
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECDSA
- **Key Takeaways**: LMS and HSS provide asymmetric authentication resistant to quantum computers without using large integer mathematics; The schemes are stateful, meaning secret key states must not be reused or security guarantees are lost; Classical APIs for digital signatures cannot be used without modification to handle dynamic secret key states; Multilevel signature schemes increase signature size and verification time but save space and time for key generation on resource-constrained systems
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: None detected
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Small private and public keys; Fast signature generation and verification; Large signatures; Moderately slow key generation compared to RSA and ECDSA
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect; Researcher
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Systems must prevent reuse of secret key states; APIs must handle dynamic secret key state updates
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: stateful-signatures; algorithms; merkle-tree-certs; pqc-101; quantum-threats
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: naturally resistant to side-channel attacks; stateful signature algorithm requiring prevention of secret key state reuse to avoid forgery
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: None detected
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: Appendix F Test Cases; interoperability between implementations ensured by sufficient detail
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: Appendix A Pseudorandom Key Generation
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: suitable for compact implementations; resource-constrained systems; small private and public keys; saves time for key generation and space for key storage
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: None detected
- **Deployment & Migration Complexity**: classical APIs for digital signatures cannot be used without modification; API MUST handle dynamic secret key state; signature-generation algorithm MUST update secret key state
- **Financial & Business Impact**: None detected
- **Organizational Readiness**: developers must take care of specific restrictions regarding stateful schemes; requires careful handling of secret keys to prevent reuse

- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: None detected
- **Migration Timeline Info**: None detected
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: None detected
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: paulmillr

- **Protocols Covered**: None detected
- **Infrastructure Layers**: None detected
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; IETF
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: FIPS 202; NIST SP 800-185
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: SHA-256; SHA-384; SHA-512; SHA-224; SHA-512/224; SHA-512/256; SHA3-224; SHA3-256; SHA3-384; SHA3-512; Keccak-224; Keccak-256; Keccak-384; Keccak-512; SHAKE128; SHAKE256; cSHAKE128; cSHAKE256; KMAC128; KMAC256; KT128; KT256; TurboSHAKE128; TurboSHAKE256; TupleHash256; ParallelHash256; KeccakPRG; BLAKE1-224; BLAKE1-256; BLAKE1-384; BLAKE1-512; BLAKE2b; BLAKE2s; BLAKE3; SHA-1; MD5; RIPEMD160; HMAC; HKDF; PBKDF2; Scrypt; Argon2d; Argon2i; Argon2id
- **Key Takeaways**: The library offers tree-shakeable and audited implementations of hash functions and KDFs for JavaScript environments; Legacy algorithms like MD5, SHA-1, and RIPEMD160 are explicitly marked as weak and unsuitable for new protocols; Scrypt and Argon2 performance in JavaScript is limited by engine constraints and lack of Uint64Array support; The library supports NIST SP 800-185 standards including cSHAKE, KMAC, and TurboSHAKE; Developers should use sub-imports to ensure small application size.
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: 2^18 effort for MD5 collisions; 2^60 effort for SHA-1 collisions; 2^80 effort for RIPEMD160 collisions; Scrypt N=2^16 to 2^24; Argon2 m=65536; maxmem limit 1GB + 1KB
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: None detected
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: 22KB gzipped for full build; 2.4KB for single-hash build; Scrypt N=2^16 takes 0.1s with 64MB RAM; Scrypt N=2^24 takes 27s with 16GB RAM; Argon2 is suggested to be avoided in JS due to lack of fast Uint64Array
- **Target Audience**: Developer; Security Architect
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: npm or deno package manager; polyfill for getRandomValues for React Native; manual maxmem adjustment for Scrypt N > 2^20
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Algorithms; Assess; code-signing; entropy-randomness; migration-program
- **Implementation Attack Surface**: DoS tests, memory safety via maxmem limits in Scrypt and Argon2, no unrolled loops to reduce source code size
- **Cryptographic Discovery & Inventory**: Algorithm enumeration of SHA2, SHA3, BLAKE1, BLAKE2, BLAKE3, legacy hashes (SHA1, MD5, RIPEMD160), MACs (HMAC, KMAC), KDFs (HKDF, PBKDF2, Scrypt, Argon2)
- **Testing & Validation Methods**: ACVP tests, fuzzing, chained/sliding window tests, conformance to RFC 4634, FIPS-202, RFC 9861, RFC 7693, RFC 3174, RFC 1321, RFC 2286, RFC 2104, RFC 5869, RFC 2898, RFC 7914, RFC 9106
- **QKD Protocols & Quantum Networking**: None detected
- **QRNG & Entropy Sources**: KeccakPRG pseudo-random generator with configurable capacity (e.g., 254 bits), randomBytes utility, entropy addition via addEntropy method
- **Constrained Device & IoT Suitability**: Tree-shakeable builds (2.4KB for single-hash), 22KB gzipped total size, support for little-endian and experimental big-endian architectures, React Native polyfill requirements, memory limits on mobile phones
- **Supply Chain & Vendor Risk**: Zero-dependency architecture, PGP-signed releases, transparent NPM builds, audit by cure53, funding by Ethereum Foundation

## HNDL Simulator (hndl-dev)

- **Reference ID**: HNDL Simulator (hndl-dev)
- **Title**: HNDL Simulator (hndl-dev)
- **Authors**: Cryptographic Discovery Platforms
- **Publication Date**: 2026-03-02
- **Last Updated**: 2026-04-11
- **Document Status**: Verified
- **Main Topic**: The paper reframes Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later attacks as an economic problem, quantifying adversary storage and quantum computation costs across TLS 1.2, TLS 1.3, QUIC, and SSH to evaluate defense-in-depth strategies like rekeying and Encrypted Client Hello.
- **PQC Algorithms Covered**: None detected
- **Quantum Threats Addressed**: Harvest-now decrypt-later; Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer; Shor's algorithm
- **Migration Timeline Info**: NIST finalized first three PQC standards in August 2024; Microsoft targets full ecosystem transition by 2033; Q-Day estimated in 2030–2040 window with 50% probability of breaking RSA-2048 within 15 years
- **Applicable Regions / Bodies**: United States; Europe; Bodies: NIST, NSA, ANSSI, BSI, NLNCSA, ETSI, IETF
- **Leaders Contributions Mentioned**: Javier Blanco-Romero; Florina Almenares Mendoza; Carlos García Rubio; Celeste Campo; Daniel Díaz Sánchez; Mosca; Piani; Cremers; Dowling; Danezis; Wittneben
- **PQC Products Mentioned**: HNDL Simulator (hndl-dev)
- **Protocols Covered**: TLS 1.2; TLS 1.3; QUIC; SSH; PQ3
- **Infrastructure Layers**: Backbone fiber taps; Internet Service Provider cooperation; Data-center presence; Cloud archive pricing
- **Standardization Bodies**: NIST; IETF; ETSI
- **Compliance Frameworks Referenced**: CNSA 2.0
- **Classical Algorithms Referenced**: RSA; ECC; DH; DHE; ECDHE; AES; chacha20-poly1305
- **Key Takeaways**: Retaining intercepted traffic is economically trivial, shifting defense focus to increasing adversary decryption costs; Encrypted Client Hello forces indiscriminate bulk collection to inflate archive size; Aggressive rekeying and larger key exchange parameters multiply quantum computations required for recovery; TLS 1.3 and QUIC lack in-band ephemeral rekeying, creating a critical protocol gap
- **Security Levels & Parameters**: RSA-2048; Level 3 messaging security (Apple PQ3)
- **Hybrid & Transition Approaches**: Hybrid key exchange; Hybrid post-quantum Transport Layer Security
- **Performance & Size Considerations**: Petabyte-scale archival costs collapsed by roughly 95% since 2010; 68 exabytes of global data traffic in 2024; Negligible bandwidth overhead for rekeying
- **Target Audience**: Security Architect; Researcher; Policy Maker; CISO
- **Implementation Prerequisites**: Open-source simulation pipeline; Encrypted Client Hello configuration; SSH RekeyLimit configuration; Larger key exchange parameters
- **Relevant PQC Today Features**: Threats, Assess, Migrate, tls-basics, vpn-ssh-pqc, pqc-risk-management, hybrid-crypto
